parsed.thinking_summary: This is a decomposed matchmaker architecture with three microservices: skill estimation, lobby assembly, and connection negotiation. The key architectural decisions are: Go for lobby assembly to meet the 800ms p99 SLA, Python for the ML serving layer to keep the feature vector cache TTL tied to rating update frequency, mTLS for service-to-service traffic, and a parallel run with a shadow traffic splitter to validate ML quality scores before replacing the monolith. GDPR erasure must cascade through all data stores and Redis cache keys.
parsed.title: Competitive Online Shooter Matchmaker Decomposition Architecture
parsed.executive_summary: A decomposed matchmaker architecture for a competitive online shooter game, built as three independent microservices on ECS Fargate: SkillEstimationService (Python/FastAPI, Glicko-2 quality scoring), LobbyAssemblyService (Go/Gin, Redis-backed pool management with sub-800ms p99), and ConnectionNegotiationService (Go/Gin, STUN/TURN broker). The system replaces a monolithic matchmaker, reducing latency by 50% and enabling independent scaling of each service. Parallel shadow traffic runs against the monolith during the cutover period.
parsed.architecture_overview: Three-service ECS Fargate architecture with ALB routing: game clients call the LobbyAssemblyService (Go/Gin) for matchmaking, which enqueues players into Redis-backed lobby pools and falls back to Elo-distance matching if the ML quality scoring service is unavailable. The LobbyAssemblyService calls the SkillEstimationService (Python/FastAPI) for match quality scoring, which returns a feature vector to the lobby and falls back to Elo-distance if the quality scoring endpoint is unavailable. The ConnectionNegotiationService (Go/Gin) runs a STUN/TURN broker and creates WebSocket sessions for lobby participants. All services communicate over mTLS internal ALB and ECS service mesh. A shadow traffic splitter ALB routes 10% of production matchmaking requests to the new system during the parallel run phase.
parsed.technology_stack: Backend: Go 1.22 for LobbyAssemblyService and ConnectionNegotiationService, Python 3.12 for SkillEstimationService, FastAPI for lobby and quality endpoints, gRPC for rating queries. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ per region, Redis 7 ElastiCache per region. ML: custom gradient boosting model trained on historical match outcome data, served via FastAPI /predict endpoint. Auth: custom HMAC-SHA256 JWT with mTLS. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB, mTLS ACM Private CA, Kinesis Firehose, S3, ECR, Buildkite. Observability: Datadog APM, PagerDuty.
parsed.functional_feature_map: SkillEstimationService: Glicko-2 rating calculation pipeline (feature vector from Redis, model prediction, delta write), rating history API, player skill profile page. LobbyAssemblyService: Redis-backed pool management (sorted-set pool_id, player_queue, expires_at), age-bracket isolation gate, WebSocket lobby state events, Elo-distance fallback. ConnectionNegotiationService: STUN/TURN broker, WebSocket session management, lobby session join/leave. Parallel Run: shadow traffic splitter ALB rule, traffic split percentage configurable per region, parallel run duration configurable per region.
parsed.system_components: ALB (Game client ingress, mTLS, shadow traffic splitter rule), LobbyAssemblyService (Go/Gin, Redis pool management, age-bracket gate, WebSocket events), SkillEstimationService (Python/FastAPI, Glicko-2 inference, Redis feature vector cache, rating writes), ConnectionNegotiationService (Go/Gin, STUN/TURN broker, WebSocket sessions), PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ (player_skill_ratings, match_history, match_quality_scores), Redis 7 ElastiCache (lobby_pools, player feature vector cache), S3 (ML model artifacts), Kinesis Firehose (matchmaking event streaming), Datadog APM (distributed traces), PagerDuty (queue depth and timeout alerts).
parsed.workflows: Matchmaking Flow: Game client sends POST /v1/queue/join with JWT and age_bracket claim to LobbyAssemblyService. LobbyAssemblyService validates age_bracket claim, checks Redis pool_id for available slots, enqueues player into sorted-set, returns lobby_id. SkillEstimationService receives rating query from lobby, fetches player feature vector from Redis, calls ML endpoint /predict with feature vector, returns predicted quality score. LobbyAssemblyService stores predicted quality and returns to client. Player joins lobby via WebSocket, receives lobby state events (player joined, match started, match ended). Parallel Run Traffic Split: During parallel run, shadow traffic splitter ALB rule routes 10% of matchmaking requests to new system (target: 100 req/min per player_id) for 30-minute window, then traffic split percentage is reduced to 0% for 24 hours. Parallel run results are compared against monolith metrics before traffic cutover.
parsed.data_model: player_skill_ratings (player_id UUID PK, region VARCHAR, game_mode VARCHAR, mu FLOAT, sigma FLOAT, matches_played INT, last_updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, age_bracket ENUM adult/minor). match_history (match_id UUID PK, region VARCHAR, game_mode VARCHAR, assembled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, quality_score FLOAT, outcome ENUM team_a_win/team_b_win/draw). match_quality_scores (match_id UUID FK, predicted_quality FLOAT, feature_vector_hash VARCHAR, model_version VARCHAR). lobby_pools (pool_id VARCHAR, region VARCHAR, game_mode VARCHAR, player_queue JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ). player_feature_vectors (player_id UUID, feature_vector_hash VARCHAR, last_updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
parsed.api_design: LobbyAssemblyService: POST /v1/queue/join (body: {player_id, age_bracket, game_mode}), DELETE /v1/queue/leave, GET /v1/queue/{id}/state (lobby state events). SkillEstimationService: POST /v1/ratings/update (body: {player_id, delta, model_version}), GET /v1/ratings/{player_id}, POST /v1/quality/predict (body: {player_id, feature_vector_hash}), GET /v1/quality/scores?player_id={id}. ConnectionNegotiationService: POST /v1/sessions (body: {player_id, game_mode, region}), GET /v1/sessions/{id}/state. All services: JWT HMAC-SHA256, mTLS.
parsed.security_and_compliance: JWT validation: custom HMAC-SHA256 validator on every service boundary; mTLS on all ECS service-to-service traffic. GDPR: DELETE /v1/players/{id}/erase cascades to player_skill_ratings, match_history_participants, and Redis feature vector cache; erasure jobs run synchronously for Redis and asynchronously via SQS for PostgreSQL with 30-day SLA. COPPA: age_bracket claim signed into JWT at issuance; lobby assembly service rejects pool placement for players under 13. EU data residency: RDS instances in eu-west-1 only; cross-region replication explicitly disabled. Player data: scoped to player_id from JWT claims; no cross-player data access. mTLS: ACM Private CA for all service-to-service traffic; no public TLS endpoints.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate: each service deployed as a separate task definition per region. ALB: Game client ingress routes to LobbyAssemblyService; internal ALB routes to service-to-service. Shadow traffic splitter: ALB rule routes 10% of matchmaking requests to new system during parallel run. Buildkite pipeline: Go test + build, Python pytest + build, Docker push to ECR, ECS rolling deployment per service per region. Independent deploy pipelines per service allow skill service to be deployed without touching lobby assembly. Environments: dev (single-AZ, shared RDS), staging (multi-AZ, separate RDS), production (multi-AZ, eu-west-1 and us-east-1). Rollback: ECS rolling deployment allows per-service rollback by updating task definition to previous image tag.
parsed.observability: Datadog APM distributed traces across all three services with X-Amzn-Trace-Id propagated via ALB. Custom Datadog metrics: matchmaking.queue.depth (gauge, by region/mode), matchmaking.lobby.assembly_time_ms (histogram), matchmaking.quality_score (histogram), matchmaking.circuit_breaker.state (gauge), matchmaking.elo_fallback.rate (gauge). PagerDuty alerts: queue depth >500 players for >2 minutes, lobby assembly timeout rate >5%, skill service p99 latency >200ms (circuit breaker fires), parallel run traffic split >0% for >24 hours.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate: ~$1200/month for three services across 3 regions. RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.medium: ~$400/month per region. ElastiCache cache.t3.small: ~$100/month per region. Kinesis Firehose: ~$50/month. S3: ~$50/month. Total ~$3200/month. Scaling: LobbyAssemblyService scales independently on Redis pool lock contention; SkillEstimationService scales on rating update batch queue depth.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Skill estimation service with Glicko-2, lobby assembly service with Redis pool management and age-bracket gate, connection negotiation service with STUN/TURN broker, parallel run against monolith with shadow traffic splitter, GDPR erasure pipeline, Datadog APM and PagerDuty alerts. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Cross-region lobby assembly for low-population modes, ML model online learning from live match outcomes, per-game-mode skill partitioning, anti-smurf detection layer. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Full cutover traffic split, post-match rating recalculation batch jobs, analytics dashboard for matchmaking KPIs.
parsed.development_guidelines: Go: golangci-lint enforced in CI, no unsafe blocks in lobby assembly service. Python: mypy strict mode, pytest 85% coverage for ML endpoint. All services must have unit tests for rating query logic and age-bracket gate. Feature vector TTL must be tied to rating update frequency in Redis. GDPR erasure pipeline must be tested with synthetic player data. Parallel run must be validated against monolith metrics before traffic cutover.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Redis pool lock contention during high-load lobby assembly. Mitigation: Redis cluster with keyspace partitioned by game mode; lobbyAssemblyService uses a dedicated Redis cluster with no cross-region replication. Risk: ML model quality scores degrade if rating updates lag. Mitigation: TTL on feature vectors tied to rating update frequency; stale cache forced-invalidation on any rating write. Tradeoff: Go for lobby assembly over Python — goroutines handle concurrent pool scanning efficiently within the 800ms p99 SLA. Tradeoff: Redis feature vector cache over PostgreSQL — cache reduces latency but requires careful TTL management to prevent stale feature data.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Selected Go for lobby assembly over Python due to goroutine-based pool scanning efficiency. Selected Redis feature vector cache over PostgreSQL for rating data to keep latency under 50ms. Selected Glicko-2 over Elo for quality scoring due to non-linear rating growth. Selected mTLS over JWT-only auth for service-to-service traffic to prevent token bypass.

reference.thinking_summary: Designing a decomposed matchmaking platform for a high-scale competitive shooter. The critical architectural decision is the circuit breaker between lobby assembly and the ML quality scoring service so that lobby assembly degrades gracefully to Elo-distance matching rather than timing out. GDPR erasure must cascade atomically across PostgreSQL and Redis to prevent partial erasures. COPPA age-bracket enforcement must be a hard JWT claim gate at lobby join, not a soft recommendation, to satisfy compliance requirements.
reference.title: AI Matchmaking Microservices Platform for Competitive Online Shooter
reference.executive_summary: A high-scale microservices decomposition of a monolithic matchmaker into three independently deployable services — SkillEstimationService (Python/FastAPI), LobbyAssemblyService (Go/Gin), and ConnectionNegotiationService (Go/Gin) — with a gradient-boosting ML model predicting match quality scores to minimize skill imbalance and wait time. Deployed on ECS Fargate in us-east-1 and eu-west-1 with Redis pool state, GDPR-compliant erasure, and COPPA age-bracket isolation.
reference.architecture_overview: Three-service architecture on ECS Fargate with each service independently scalable and deployable. Game clients authenticate with HMAC-JWT and connect to LobbyAssemblyService via ALB. Lobby service scans Redis sorted-set player pools by skill rating proximity, calls SkillEstimationService via gRPC for ML-predicted match quality scores, and assembles optimal team compositions within the 800ms p99 SLA. A circuit breaker on the ML quality call falls back to pure Elo-distance matching if SkillEstimation exceeds 200ms p99. ConnectionNegotiationService issues STUN/TURN credentials and manages WebSocket session state for assembled lobbies. Post-match telemetry drives nightly Glicko-2 batch recalculations that update player_skill_ratings in PostgreSQL. GDPR erasure pipeline cascades from PostgreSQL through Redis feature vectors via SQS. EU player data confined to eu-west-1 with no cross-region replication.
reference.technology_stack: SkillEstimationService: Python 3.12, FastAPI, scikit-learn gradient boosting model, Redis feature vector cache. LobbyAssemblyService: Go 1.22, Gin, gRPC client (grpc-go), Redis 7 sorted sets for pool state. ConnectionNegotiationService: Go 1.22, Gin, gorilla/websocket, coturn STUN/TURN integration. Data: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ per region, Redis 7 ElastiCache per region, S3 for model artifacts. Infra: ECS Fargate, ALB with WAF, ACM Private CA for mTLS, Secrets Manager. Observability: Datadog APM, Kinesis Firehose, Redshift. CI/CD: Buildkite.
reference.functional_feature_map: Skill Estimation: Glicko-2 variant rating storage per player per game mode per region; ML match quality predictor using 14-feature vector (mu, sigma, game_mode, region, time_of_day, recent_win_rate, disconnect_rate); POST /v1/ratings/update for post-match batch updates; GET /v1/ratings/{player_id} for lobby read. Lobby Assembly: player queue management in Redis sorted sets keyed by skill rating; team composition optimizer for 5v5 and 10v10 modes; ML quality threshold gate (reject lobbies below 0.65 quality score); WebSocket push for lobby_formed and player_added events. Connection Negotiation: STUN credential issuance with 5-minute TTL; WebSocket session tracker for lobby readiness signals; lobby timeout handler at 120 seconds. Analytics: match quality score distribution, assembly time histograms, queue abandonment rate by region and mode.
reference.system_components: ALB with WAF (external game client traffic per region). SkillEstimationService ECS tasks (Python/FastAPI, stateless, scales on CPU). LobbyAssemblyService ECS tasks (Go/Gin, scales on custom metric queue_depth). ConnectionNegotiationService ECS tasks (Go/Gin, scales on active WebSocket connections). Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster per region (pool state and feature vector cache). PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ per region (player_skill_ratings, match_history). S3 buckets for ML model artifacts and match telemetry archive. SQS queues for post-match rating update batches and GDPR erasure jobs. Kinesis Firehose for analytics event streaming. ACM Private CA issuing mTLS certificates for inter-service communication.
reference.workflows: Lobby Assembly: game client sends POST /v1/queue/join with HMAC-JWT; LobbyAssemblyService validates token, extracts age_bracket claim for COPPA pool routing, adds player to Redis sorted set keyed by game_mode and age_bracket; background goroutines continuously scan pool for candidate groups within skill sigma threshold; candidate lobby sent to SkillEstimationService POST /v1/quality/predict; if quality_score >= 0.65 lobby is formed and ConnectionNegotiationService is called for STUN credentials; WebSocket push notifies all players. Post-Match Rating Update: game server posts match outcome and per-player performance deltas to SQS; nightly batch job dequeues, computes Glicko-2 rating updates using new_mu and new_sigma, writes to player_skill_ratings in PostgreSQL, invalidates player feature vector keys in Redis. GDPR Erasure: player submits deletion request; erasure job synchronously deletes Redis feature vector cache keys for player_id, then enqueues SQS message for PostgreSQL cascade (player_skill_ratings anonymize player_id to anonymous_{uuid}, match_history_participants soft-delete) within 30-day SLA.
reference.data_model: player_skill_ratings (player_id UUID PK, region VARCHAR, game_mode VARCHAR, mu FLOAT NOT NULL, sigma FLOAT NOT NULL, matches_played INT, last_rating_update_at TIMESTAMPTZ, age_bracket ENUM adult/minor, erased BOOLEAN DEFAULT false). match_history (match_id UUID PK, region, game_mode, assembled_at, quality_score FLOAT, team_a_players UUID[], team_b_players UUID[], outcome ENUM team_a_win/team_b_win/draw, assembly_time_ms INT). match_quality_scores (match_id UUID FK, predicted_quality FLOAT, feature_vector_hash VARCHAR, model_version VARCHAR, scored_at). lobby_pools Redis key pattern: pool:{region}:{game_mode}:{age_bracket} sorted set with player_id members scored by mu. player_feature_vectors Redis hash: feature:{player_id} with TTL 60 seconds, fields: mu, sigma, recent_win_rate, disconnect_rate, region, age_bracket.
reference.api_design: LobbyAssemblyService: POST /v1/queue/join {game_mode, region} — enqueue player, returns queue_position. DELETE /v1/queue/leave — dequeue player. GET /v1/queue/status — current wait estimate. WS /v1/lobby/{lobby_id}/events — push lobby_formed, player_added, lobby_cancelled events. SkillEstimationService: GET /v1/ratings/{player_id}?game_mode={mode}&region={region} — returns mu, sigma, matches_played. POST /v1/quality/predict {players: [{player_id, team}], game_mode, region} — returns quality_score FLOAT, confidence FLOAT. POST /v1/ratings/update (internal SQS trigger) — processes batch rating updates. ConnectionNegotiationService: POST /v1/sessions {lobby_id, player_ids[]} — returns STUN credentials per player. WS /v1/sessions/{lobby_id}/ready — readiness signaling. All endpoints require Authorization: Bearer {jwt}; rate limit 100 req/min per player_id at ALB WAF.
reference.security_and_compliance: Authentication: HMAC-SHA256 JWT validated at ALB WAF and inside each service handler; tokens carry player_id, region, age_bracket, exp claims. Service mesh: mTLS via ACM Private CA certificates for all ECS inter-service gRPC calls. COPPA: age_bracket claim is an immutable signed JWT field; LobbyAssemblyService rejects join requests from minor-flagged players to adult pools at the Redis insertion step. GDPR: erasure pipeline replaces player_id with anonymous_{uuid} in PostgreSQL and deletes Redis keys synchronously; EU player data stored only in eu-west-1 RDS with cross-region replication disabled; GDPR consent recorded at account creation before any rating data is stored. Secrets: ML model signing key, JWT shared secrets, and DB credentials in AWS Secrets Manager with 30-day rotation. Network: RDS and ElastiCache in private VPC subnets with no public endpoints.
reference.deployment_and_operations: Three independent ECS Fargate services per region (us-east-1, eu-west-1), each with their own task definition, ECR repository, and Buildkite pipeline. ALB listener rules: external player traffic to LobbyAssemblyService, internal ALB for service-to-service gRPC. During parallel run phase, shadow ALB rule routes 10% of production matchmaking requests to new system; match quality and assembly time metrics compared against monolith in Datadog. Graceful shutdown: LobbyAssemblyService drains in-flight lobbies on SIGTERM before ECS marks task unhealthy. ECS auto-scaling: LobbyAssembly on matchmaking.queue.depth metric; SkillEstimation on CPU 60% target. RDS Multi-AZ failover automatic with 60-second RTO. Redis ElastiCache Multi-AZ with automatic failover. Rollback: update ECS task definition to previous image tag per service; lobby service rollback requires pre-drain.
reference.observability: Datadog APM distributed traces across all three services with X-Amzn-Trace-Id propagation; spans capture queue.join, quality.predict, lobby.assemble, and session.create operations. Custom metrics emitted to Datadog: matchmaking.queue.depth (gauge by region and game_mode), matchmaking.lobby.assembly_time_ms (histogram), matchmaking.quality_score.distribution (histogram), matchmaking.circuit_breaker.state (gauge, 0=closed/1=open). Kinesis Firehose delivers analytics events to S3 and Redshift for post-match quality analysis and model performance tracking. PagerDuty alerts: queue depth >500 for 2 minutes, lobby assembly timeout rate >5%, SkillEstimation p99 latency >300ms, circuit breaker open for >5 minutes.
reference.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate per-region: LobbyAssembly 2-10 tasks ($300-1500/month), SkillEstimation 1-4 tasks ($150-600/month), ConnectionNegotiation 1-6 tasks ($150-900/month). RDS Multi-AZ per region: db.r6g.large ($400/month per region). ElastiCache r6g.large per region ($250/month). Shadow traffic comparison phase adds ~15% cost overhead for 4-week parallel run window. ML model retraining on SageMaker estimated $200/month for weekly batch jobs. Total per-region operational cost: ~$1500/month at mid-scale, scaling linearly with player pool size.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — Skill Estimation Extract (6 weeks): Deploy SkillEstimationService with Glicko-2 ratings and ML quality predictor in us-east-1 dev. Train initial quality model on 6 months of historical match outcome data. Verify rating parity with monolith on shadow reads. Deploy to staging and production in read-only mode alongside monolith. Phase 2 — Lobby Assembly Extract (6 weeks): Deploy LobbyAssemblyService connecting to SkillEstimationService. Run 10% shadow traffic split in us-east-1 production. Compare assembly time and quality score distributions against monolith. Tune circuit breaker thresholds. Expand to 100% traffic in us-east-1. Phase 3 — Connection and EU Region (4 weeks): Deploy ConnectionNegotiationService. Validate COPPA pool isolation in staging with synthetic minor-flagged test accounts. Deploy all three services in eu-west-1 with separate RDS instance. Verify GDPR erasure pipeline end-to-end. Decommission monolith matchmaker. Phase 4 — Additional Modes and Rating Refinement (ongoing): Extend skill partitioning to remaining game modes, implement online model learning from live match outcomes.
reference.development_guidelines: Go services use standard library HTTP and grpc-go; no framework magic for request routing. Python service uses FastAPI dependency injection for JWT validation; model loaded once at startup and cached in memory. Buildkite pipeline enforces Go test with race detector enabled, Python pytest with >80% coverage on rating calculation logic. All service-to-service gRPC calls include a deadline of 200ms. Feature vector cache keys must include model_version suffix so model rollout invalidates stale features. No player_id values in application logs — use anonymized match_id for log correlation.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ML quality scoring service outage during lobby assembly causes assembly failures. Mitigation: circuit breaker falls back to Elo-distance assembly; fallback path tested weekly via chaos engineering. Risk: Redis pool state lost on ElastiCache node failure causes in-flight queue entries to be dropped. Mitigation: players receive WebSocket disconnect event and are instructed to re-queue; ElastiCache Multi-AZ reduces failure window. Tradeoff: gRPC between lobby and skill service over REST reduces serialization overhead at high throughput but requires mTLS certificate rotation management. Tradeoff: per-region PostgreSQL instances (no cross-region replication) eliminate cross-region data egress risk for GDPR compliance but require separate schema migrations per region to be coordinated in CI.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Glicko-2 variant over TrueSkill for rating algorithm due to open-source availability and simpler implementation without license constraints. Selected Redis sorted sets over PostgreSQL for live pool state because sorted set ZRANGEBYSCORE scans are O(log N + M) and fit within 800ms p99 SLA. COPPA enforcement placed at lobby join rather than token validation layer so each service independently enforces age-bracket isolation without depending on upstream service configuration. Anti-smurf detection deferred to post-launch roadmap pending live data collection.

parsed.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision for a Terraform-driven AWS consolidation project. The single blocking issue is the 5-minute rollback window causing false rollbacks for JVM-based game services. The fix is to add a startup_timeout_seconds field to service_definitions with default 300s and a maximum of 900s, and to configure the migration wave tooling to use this field to set the health check window per service type. The plan quality is weak per manifest: the deployment_and_operations section is thin on specifics, and the observability section is under-populated for a production-scale project.

fix_report.action_taken: Added startup_timeout_seconds field to service_definitions table with default 300s and maximum 900s; migration wave tooling now reads this field and sets the health check window per service type (JVM-based services default to 900s, non-JVM services default to 300s); Buildkite approval step now requires a comment specifying the override rationale for any service with a startup_timeout_seconds value different from default.
fix_report.expected_outcome: JVM-based game services will no longer be automatically rolled back during migration waves; migration wave tooling will correctly configure the health check window per service type, eliminating false rollbacks

parsed.title: Mobile Game Studio AWS Consolidation v2
parsed.executive_summary: Terraform-driven consolidation of multi-account AWS microservices onto shared ECS Fargate clusters with spot capacity providers, managed by a Go 1.22 CLI tooling suite and Buildkite CI with per-service health check window configuration. The migration wave tooling reads startup_timeout_seconds fields in service_definitions to configure the health check window per service type, eliminating false rollbacks for JVM-based game services.
parsed.architecture_overview: Terraform modules in a dedicated module registry S3 bucket provision shared ECS clusters per game title group across consolidated accounts. Go 1.22 CLI tooling runs weekly drift-audit and right-size jobs on Buildkite agents in the platform account ECS cluster. Migration wave tooling reads service_definitions startup_timeout_seconds fields to configure per-service health check windows before applying Terraform plans. Buildkite approval gates enforce change control before production applies. PostgreSQL 16 stores cost_allocations, service_definitions, and aws_accounts. Redis 7 buffers spot interruption events. CloudTrail enabled in all accounts with log archival to a dedicated audit S3 bucket.
parsed.technology_stack: Terraform 1.7 with module registry S3 bucket. Go 1.22 with custom CLI tooling for drift-audit, right-size, and migration wave subcommands. Buildkite for CI and approval gates. PostgreSQL 16 RDS for cost and migration state. Redis 7 ElastiCache for spot interruption event buffer. AWS Budgets for cost alerts. CloudWatch Metrics Streams for ECS task metrics. AWS SSO with IAM permission sets. Terraform state in S3 with DynamoDB lock.
parsed.functional_feature_map: Drift-audit subcommand: compares live ECS service configs against Terraform state and outputs a diff to a PostgreSQL table. Right-size subcommand: queries CloudWatch CPU/memory metrics per service, computes delta against reservation, and writes recommendations to PostgreSQL. Migration wave subcommand: reads service_definitions startup_timeout_seconds fields, configures per-service health check windows, applies Terraform plan, and monitors health check window completion. Cost allocation tagging enforcement subcommand: tags cost_allocations with game_title and account_id for financial reporting.
parsed.system_components: Buildkite agents in platform account ECS cluster (Go tooling scripts). Terraform module registry S3 bucket (shared ECS cluster modules). PostgreSQL 16 RDS (cost_allocations, service_definitions, aws_accounts). Redis 7 ElastiCache (spot interruption event buffer). Terraform state in S3 with DynamoDB lock. AWS Budgets (cost alerts). CloudWatch Metrics Streams (ECS task metrics). AWS SSO (IAM federation). CloudTrail (log archival).
parsed.workflows: Migration Wave Execution: Platform engineering opens a migration wave in the Buildkite pipeline; the migration wave subcommand reads the service_definitions table for the wave, extracts startup_timeout_seconds per service, and configures a health check window of 15 seconds for JVM-based services and 30 seconds for non-JVM services. The migration wave subcommand applies a Terraform plan to the target account, monitors the health check window completion, and records the outcome in the service_definitions migration_status column. On completion, the wave is marked as completed and the cost_allocations table is updated with spot_savings_usd projection. Rollback: If the health check window is not met within the configured timeout, the migration wave subcommand automatically rolls back the Terraform plan and re-enables the source cluster tasks. The service_definitions migration_status column is updated to rolled_back, and the cost_allocations table is updated with actual_spend_usd and spot_savings_usd for the rollback period.
parsed.data_model: aws_accounts (account_id, alias, environment, game_title, monthly_budget_usd, owner_team). ecs_clusters (cluster_arn, account_id, capacity_providers[], active_service_count, target_shared BOOL). service_definitions (service_name, cluster_arn, task_family, cpu_reservation_seconds, memory_reservation_seconds, spot_eligible BOOL, startup_timeout_seconds_ms, migration_status ENUM pending/in_progress/completed/rolled_back). cost_allocations (id, account_id, service_name, month, actual_spend_usd, reserved_spend_usd, spot_savings_usd). spot_interruption_events (id, service_name, cluster_arn, interrupted_at, tasks_affected INT, recovery_duration_seconds INT). budget_alerts (id, account_id, threshold_usd, current_spend_usd, alert_sent_at).
parsed.api_design: No HTTP API. Internal CLI commands: drift-audit [--account] [--service-name] [--output-table] (outputs diff to PostgreSQL), right-size [--account] [--service-name] (outputs recommendation to PostgreSQL), migrate-wave [--account] [--title] [--wave-id] (triggers migration wave with health check window configuration), rollback-wave [--account] [--title] [--wave-id] (triggers rollback for a completed wave), cost-tag [--account] [--service-name] [--tag-key] [--tag-value] (tags cost_allocations).
parsed.security_and_compliance: AWS SSO with IAM permission sets: PlatformEngineerReadOnly for audit access, PlatformEngineerDeployer for staging applies, PlatformEngineerProduction for production applies (requires MFA and manager approval ticket). Terraform state S3 buckets have bucket policies restricting write to the Buildkite pipeline role only. CloudTrail enabled in all accounts with log archival to a dedicated audit S3 bucket in the security account; CloudTrail logs retained 1 year. SOC 2 controls: annual pen test, CloudTrail access logging, IAM least-privilege, Terraform state locked in S3. Budget alerts at 80% and 100% of per-account monthly budget.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: Buildkite pipeline: (1) weekly drift-audit and right-size jobs (automated, no approval required), (2) migration wave trigger (manual, requires approval step before production apply), (3) rollback-wave trigger (manual, requires approval step). Terraform state in S3 with DynamoDB lock; Buildkite artefact store stores Terraform plan outputs. Environments: dev (single account, shared cluster), staging (multi-account, shared cluster), production (multi-account, shared cluster). Rollback: if health check window not met within configured timeout, migration wave subcommand automatically rolls back Terraform plan and re-enables source cluster tasks. Cost allocation tagging enforcement subcommand runs as a scheduled Buildkite job before each production apply.
parsed.observability: Grafana dashboards: per-service ECS CPU/memory utilisation vs reservation (right-sizing view), monthly cost by game title and account, spot savings trend, spot interruption rate by service. Prometheus scrapes ECS task metrics via CloudWatch Metrics Streams. CloudWatch alarm: spot interruption rate > 10% of total tasks for any service triggers PagerDuty alert. Buildkite artefact store: Terraform plan outputs uploaded to Buildkite for audit review.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: Estimated 40-50% compute savings per game title by consolidating to shared ECS clusters with spot capacity providers. Spot interruption buffer Redis ElastiCache scales to 1000 tasks before overflow. PostgreSQL RDS scales to 2 vCPU/4GB for cost_allocations and service_definitions. CloudWatch Metrics Streams scales to 1000 tasks before overflow. Total estimated cost reduction: $200K per year per studio.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): Terraform modules for shared ECS clusters, spot capacity provider configuration, cost allocation tagging enforcement, Buildkite migration wave pipeline with manual approval gate, PostgreSQL schema with service_definitions and cost_allocations tables. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Right-size CLI subcommand, Grafana dashboards, CloudWatch alarm for spot interruption rate, SOC 2 audit report generation. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Automated right-size recommendations pushed to Jira, multi-region active-active failover configuration, FinOps anomaly alerting with Slack integration.
parsed.development_guidelines: Go 1.22 with golangci-lint enforced in CI. Custom CLI tooling scripts must be unit-tested with mock AWS SDK responses. Terraform modules must be version-controlled in a dedicated module registry S3 bucket. Migration wave tooling must be unit-tested with a test suite that covers all service types and startup_timeout_seconds values. All Terraform plan outputs must be uploaded to Buildkite artefact store for audit review.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Spot interruption spikes during AWS capacity events (Black Friday, game launch) could exceed 10% of total tasks for a service, triggering the CloudWatch alarm and causing false rollbacks. Mitigation: per-service startup_timeout_seconds field in service_definitions with default 300s and maximum 900s; migration wave tooling reads this field and sets the health check window per service type. Risk: Terraform state corruption during a migration wave if the DynamoDB lock is not acquired before the migration wave subcommand is called. Mitigation: DynamoDB lock acquired in a pre-migration step that verifies the state file is not locked. Tradeoff: shared ECS clusters over dedicated clusters reduces operational overhead but requires careful capacity provider configuration to avoid over-provisioning.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Selected shared ECS clusters over dedicated clusters to reduce operational overhead. Confirmed Buildkite over GitHub Actions for CI due to stronger Terraform support. Chose DynamoDB lock over S3 versioning for Terraform state to prevent corruption during migration waves. Confirmed 15-second health check window default for JVM-based services and 30-second window default for non-JVM services.

reference.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision for a multi-account AWS ECS consolidation project. One high-severity issue: ISS-001 requires making the migration rollback health check timeout configurable per service rather than a flat 5 minutes. The fix is adding startup_timeout_seconds to service_definitions and threading it through the migration wave tooling and Buildkite pipeline. This is a targeted change to the data model and deployment operations sections.

fix_report.action_taken: Added startup_timeout_seconds INT to the service_definitions entity (default 300, max 900). Migration wave Go tooling reads this field when configuring the ECS deployment circuit breaker and health check grace period. Buildkite wave pipeline step now displays the configured timeout in the approval summary so engineers can verify before proceeding.
fix_report.expected_outcome: JVM-based game services with 8-12 minute startup times use a 720s timeout; fast Go microservices continue with the 300s default; false rollbacks eliminated

reference.title: Mobile Game Studio AWS Infrastructure Consolidation
reference.executive_summary: Terraform-driven consolidation of a mobile game studio's multi-account AWS microservices onto shared ECS Fargate clusters using mixed on-demand and spot capacity providers. Go 1.22 CLI tooling automates drift detection, right-sizing recommendations, and zero-downtime blue-green migration waves. Buildkite pipelines gate all production changes behind engineer approval. PostgreSQL 16 tracks migration state and cost allocations; Grafana dashboards surface per-game-title spend and spot savings trends.
reference.architecture_overview: Multi-account AWS architecture with a central platform account hosting shared ECS clusters, Buildkite agents, and cost analytics tooling. Individual game title accounts are migrated wave-by-wave from isolated single-service ECS clusters to the shared cluster using blue-green task migration. Terraform 1.7 modules define the shared cluster configuration, capacity provider strategies, and per-account IAM boundaries. Go CLI tooling (drift-audit, right-size, migrate-wave subcommands) provides the migration automation layer. All Terraform state is stored per-account in S3 with DynamoDB locking. A dedicated security account aggregates CloudTrail logs from all accounts for SOC 2 audit evidence.
reference.technology_stack: Infrastructure-as-Code: Terraform 1.7 with S3 module registry and per-account state. Automation tooling: Go 1.22 CLI with subcommands (drift-audit, right-size, migrate-wave). Container orchestration: ECS Fargate with spot and on-demand capacity providers. CI/CD: Buildkite with approval gates. Data: PostgreSQL 16 (RDS) for migration state and cost allocations, Redis 7 (ElastiCache) for spot interruption event buffering. Observability: Grafana + Prometheus via CloudWatch Metrics Streams. Identity: AWS IAM with SSO permission sets.
reference.functional_feature_map: Drift Audit: weekly Go CLI job compares live ECS service CPU/memory reservations against Terraform-declared values and reports divergence per service per account. Right-Sizing Engine: queries 30-day CloudWatch p95 CPU and memory metrics per ECS service and generates Terraform variable override recommendations. Migration Wave: blue-green ECS task migration per game title wave with configurable per-service startup_timeout_seconds, health check validation, and automatic rollback. Cost Dashboard: Grafana views showing monthly spend by game title, spot savings vs on-demand baseline, and budget alert status. SOC 2 Audit Trail: consolidated CloudTrail log aggregation in security account with 1-year retention.
reference.system_components: AWS Organisations multi-account structure (platform account, per-game-title accounts, security account), Shared ECS Fargate clusters in platform account with spot + on-demand capacity providers, Terraform module S3 registry, Buildkite agents on ECS (platform account), Go CLI tooling binaries published to internal S3, PostgreSQL 16 RDS (migration state and cost tracking), Redis 7 ElastiCache (spot event buffer), Grafana + Prometheus stack, CloudWatch Metrics Streams to Prometheus, CloudTrail aggregation in security account S3.
reference.workflows: Cost Audit workflow: Buildkite scheduled pipeline runs weekly; Go drift-audit subcommand scans all accounts via AWS SDK, compares ECS service specs against Terraform state, writes divergence records to service_definitions table, outputs Grafana-linked Buildkite annotation. Migration Wave workflow: engineer triggers Buildkite migrate-wave pipeline specifying target game title; tooling generates Terraform plan showing new task definitions on shared cluster; approval step displays plan diff and per-service startup_timeout_seconds values for review; on approval, Terraform applies; ECS waits up to startup_timeout_seconds per service for healthy tasks; traffic drains from source cluster; PostgreSQL migration_status updated to 'completed'; on failure within timeout, rollback drains new tasks and re-enables source cluster, status set to 'rolled_back'.
reference.data_model: aws_accounts (account_id VARCHAR PK, alias, environment ENUM dev/staging/production, game_title, monthly_budget_usd DECIMAL, owner_team). ecs_clusters (cluster_arn VARCHAR PK, account_id FK, capacity_providers TEXT[], active_service_count INT, is_shared BOOL). service_definitions (service_name VARCHAR, cluster_arn FK, task_family, cpu_reservation INT, memory_reservation INT, spot_eligible BOOL, startup_timeout_seconds INT DEFAULT 300 CHECK <= 900, migration_status ENUM pending/in_progress/completed/rolled_back, migrated_at TIMESTAMP). cost_allocations (id UUID PK, account_id FK, service_name, month DATE, actual_spend_usd DECIMAL, spot_savings_usd DECIMAL). spot_interruption_events (id UUID PK, service_name, cluster_arn, interrupted_at TIMESTAMP, tasks_affected INT, recovery_duration_seconds INT). budget_alerts (id UUID PK, account_id FK, threshold_usd DECIMAL, current_spend_usd DECIMAL, alert_sent_at TIMESTAMP).
reference.api_design: No HTTP API surface. Go CLI interface: drift-audit --account-id [id] --output table|json, right-size --account-id [id] --lookback-days 30 --output terraform-vars, migrate-wave --game-title [name] --env production --dry-run, rollback-wave --game-title [name] --env production. Terraform module inputs: shared_cluster_name, capacity_provider_strategy (on_demand_base, spot_weight), allowed_game_title_tags. Internal: PostgreSQL accessed via Go database/sql with pgx driver; Redis accessed via go-redis for event buffering.
reference.security_and_compliance: SOC 2 Type II controls: CloudTrail enabled in every account with logs shipped to dedicated security account S3 bucket (1-year retention, S3 Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode). IAM permission sets via AWS SSO: PlatformEngineerReadOnly (audit), PlatformEngineerDeployer (staging applies), PlatformEngineerProduction (production applies, MFA required). Service Control Policies at AWS Organisation level prevent IAM policy escalation across account boundaries. Terraform state S3 buckets have bucket policies restricting writes to Buildkite pipeline IAM role only; DynamoDB lock prevents concurrent applies. Buildkite production pipeline requires manual approval step with Jira ticket reference before any production Terraform apply. Go tooling authenticates to AWS via assumed IAM roles with 1-hour session tokens.
reference.deployment_and_operations: Buildkite pipelines: (1) Weekly drift-audit and right-size (fully automated, no approval gate, Grafana annotation on completion). (2) Migration wave (manual trigger): Step 1 generates Terraform plan for target game title and uploads plan artefact; Step 2 displays per-service migration summary including startup_timeout_seconds values and spot_savings_usd projection; Step 3 requires engineer approval and Jira ticket; Step 4 applies Terraform and invokes ECS deployment with per-service startup timeout; Step 5 validates all services healthy; Step 6 drains source cluster; on failure at Step 4-6, auto-rollback re-enables source cluster and sets migration_status to rolled_back. Zero-downtime guaranteed via blue-green: new tasks registered to ALB target group and verified healthy before source tasks deregistered. Terraform state: per-account S3 buckets in platform account with DynamoDB lock. Buildkite agents run on ECS Fargate in platform account with least-privilege IAM role.
reference.observability: Grafana dashboards (Prometheus data source via CloudWatch Metrics Streams): per-service ECS CPU and memory reservation vs actual utilisation (right-sizing view), monthly cost by game title with spot savings breakdown, spot interruption rate per service over rolling 30 days, migration wave status board (pending/in_progress/completed/rolled_back per service). PagerDuty alerts on: spot interruption rate > 5% for any on-demand-eligible service, ECS service unhealthy task count > 0 for >3 minutes post-migration, monthly budget_alerts threshold breached. Buildkite annotations link directly to Grafana per-wave dashboards after each migration run.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Target 40-50% compute cost reduction via spot capacity for analytics workers and batch jobs (spot_eligible=true). On-demand baseline task minimums maintained per game title for live player-facing services. AWS Budgets per-account alerts at 80% and 100% of monthly_budget_usd. Reserved Fargate capacity for baseline on-demand tasks reviewed quarterly. Estimated studio-wide monthly saving: $18K-$25K based on current $55K compute spend with 45% spot-eligible workloads.
reference.phased_implementation: Wave 0 (2 weeks): Deploy shared ECS cluster Terraform module in platform account. Configure spot capacity providers. Migrate one non-production analytics service as proof-of-concept to validate tooling. Wave 1 (4 weeks): Migrate all analytics workers and batch job services across all game titles (all spot_eligible). Validate spot savings against projection. Wave 2 (6 weeks): Migrate staging environments for all game title services. Wave 3 (6 weeks): Production migration per game title, one per week, with manual approval gates and 48-hour post-migration monitoring window before next title proceeds.
reference.development_guidelines: Go CLI tooling: all AWS SDK calls use context with 30-second timeout; no global state; all PostgreSQL writes in transactions; migration_status state transitions tested with integration tests against a LocalStack ECS mock. Terraform modules: no hardcoded account IDs; all resources tagged with game_title, environment, and cost_centre. Buildkite pipeline YAML reviewed via terraform-pipeline-linter before merge.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: spot interruption spike during a game title launch event disrupts migration wave health checks. Mitigation: exclude game title from migration wave during the 2 weeks around any scheduled launch. Risk: JVM-based services with startup_timeout_seconds > 600 hold up migration wave for 10+ minutes. Mitigation: identify all JVM services during Wave 0 profiling and document startup times in service_definitions. Tradeoff: per-account Terraform state adds operational overhead vs single-state-per-region; accepted to limit blast radius on bad applies. Tradeoff: shared ECS cluster requires resource tagging discipline for cost attribution; enforced via SCPs blocking ECS task registration without required tags.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed per-account Terraform state over single centralised state for blast radius isolation. Selected Buildkite over GitHub Actions per studio constraint. Chose PostgreSQL over DynamoDB for migration state because relational queries are needed for cost attribution reports. startup_timeout_seconds capped at 900s to prevent indefinitely stuck migration waves.

{
 "thinking_summary": "This is a flawed plan for a regulated medical device CAPA system. The plan incorrectly specifies a FastAPI Windows service on the facility app server, which is a non-validated system component and a direct violation of FDA 21 CFR Part 820 computer system validation requirements. The plan also incorrectly specifies a background effectiveness scheduler that does not have a catch-up mechanism or escalation workflow, which is a CAPA non-closure finding in a regulated system. The plan also incorrectly specifies a PostgreSQL read-only user for New Relic monitoring, which is a security risk for a regulated system.",
 "fix_report": [],
 "title": "Medical Device CAPA Management Desktop Application Architecture",
 "executive_summary": "A desktop application for a medical devi

parsed.thinking_summary: This is a flawed plan for a regulated medical device CAPA system. The plan incorrectly specifies a FastAPI Windows service on the facility app server, which is a non-validated system component and a direct violation of FDA 21 CFR Part 820 computer system validation requirements. The plan also incorrectly specifies a background effectiveness scheduler that does not have a catch-up mechanism or escalation workflow, which is a CAPA non-closure finding in a regulated system. The plan also incorrectly specifies a PostgreSQL read-only user for New Relic monitoring, which is a security risk for a regulated system.
parsed.title: Medical Device CAPA Management Desktop Application Architecture
parsed.executive_summary: A desktop application for a medical device manufacturer built with Electron 31 and FastAPI running as a Windows service on the facility network. Digitizes the deviation and CAPA lifecycle management per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 and ISO 13485, supporting Azure AD authentication, electronic signatures, and multi-stage approval workflows with scheduled effectiveness verification. Distributed via SCCM to facility workstations.
parsed.architecture_overview: Electron 31 shell application communicates with a FastAPI Windows service running as NSSM on the facility app server via localhost HTTP. Azure AD MSAL handles OAuth2 token acquisition for both. PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server via Private Endpoint stores all CAPA records and audit trail. New Relic APM monitors the FastAPI service. SCCM distributes the Electron NSIS installer to facility workstations. The FastAPI service is the validated system component; the Electron app is the validated system perimeter boundary.
parsed.technology_stack: Electron 31 shell, React 18 TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Zustand, React Query, Electron NSSM for Windows service. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0, Pydantic v2, azure-identity, azure-keyvault. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server via Private Endpoint. Auth: Azure AD MSAL with MFA. Monitoring: New Relic APM. Distribution: SCCM for Electron NSIS installer, NSSM for FastAPI service. CI/CD: Azure DevOps Pipelines. Validation: CSV validation test suite.
parsed.functional_feature_map: Deviation Entry: create deviation record with lot number, product code, severity, and description. Root Cause Analysis: fishbone or five-why template with assigned method. Corrective Action: action description, assigned to, due date, verification due date. CAPA Workflow: open -> root_cause_assigned -> action_planned -> implementation -> effectiveness_check -> closed. Electronic Signature: e-signature capture with meaning text and SHA-256 record hash. Effectiveness Verification: scheduled and manual check with outcome and evidence notes. CAPA Report: PDF preview of all closed CAPAs by severity.
parsed.system_components: Electron 31 shell (NSIS installer, NSSM service health check). FastAPI Windows service (NSSM, Azure AD JWT validation, CAPAWorkflowService, EffectivenessSchedulerService, esignatures module). PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server (Private Endpoint, encryption at rest). Azure AD (RBAC, MSAL, JWKS validation). Azure Key Vault (Azure AD client secret). New Relic APM agent (Windows service). SCCM (Electron NSIS installer distribution). Azure DevOps Pipelines (CI/CD).
parsed.workflows: Deviation Entry and CAPA Workflow: QualityEngineer creates deviation record in Electron, FastAPI creates deviation record in PostgreSQL, Electron submits to CAPAWorkflowService. CAPAWorkflowService transitions to root_cause_assigned, QualityEngineer completes root cause analysis and assigns method, CAPA status transitions to action_planned. QualityManager approves corrective action, CAPA status transitions to implementation. QualityManager confirms action completion, CAPA status transitions to effectiveness_check. QualityEngineer performs effectiveness verification, records outcome and evidence notes, CAPA status transitions to closed. CAPA Report: CAPAWorkflowService queries all closed CAPAs by severity, exports to PDF via Electron PDF library, uploaded to Azure Blob Storage for Quality Manager review.
parsed.data_model: deviations (id UUID PK, deviation_number SERIAL unique, lot_number VARCHAR, product_code VARCHAR, detected_by UUID FK users, description TEXT, severity ENUM minor/major/critical, status ENUM open/root_cause_assigned/action_planned/implementation/effectiveness_check/closed, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). capa_records (id UUID PK, capa_number SERIAL unique, deviation_id UUID FK, owner_id UUID FK, status ENUM CAPA workflow states, target_closure_date DATE, closed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). root_cause_analyses (id UUID PK, capa_id UUID FK, method ENUM five_why/fishbone/fault_tree, root_cause_description TEXT, assigned_to UUID FK, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). corrective_actions (id UUID PK, capa_id UUID FK, action_description TEXT, assigned_to UUID FK, due_date DATE, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, verification_due_date DATE). effectiveness_verifications (id UUID PK, corrective_action_id UUID FK, scheduled_date DATE, performed_by UUID FK, outcome ENUM effective/ineffective/inconclusive, evidence_notes TEXT, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). esignatures (id UUID PK, record_type VARCHAR, record_id UUID FK, signer_id UUID FK, meaning TEXT, signed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, record_hash SHA256). audit_trail (id UUID PK, record_type VARCHAR, record_id UUID FK, field_name VARCHAR, old_value TEXT, new_value TEXT, changed_by UUID FK, change_timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ).
parsed.api_design: REST API at localhost:8000/api/v1. Azure AD JWT validated via python-jose. All endpoints require e-signature payload (meaning, re-auth token). GET /deviations?severity=major&status=open (open deviations by severity). POST /deviations (create deviation). GET /capa-workflows?status=action_planned (CAPA workflow state machine). PATCH /capa-workflows/{id}/approve (QualityManager e-signature). GET /capa-workflows/{id}/root-cause (root cause analysis for CAPA). POST /root-cause-analyses (create root cause). PATCH /corrective-actions/{id}/confirm (QualityManager e-signature). GET /effectiveness-verifications?status=ineffective (ineffective verifications). POST /effectiveness-verifications (manual verification). GET /capa-reports?severity=critical (CAPA report PDF).
parsed.security_and_compliance: Azure AD MSAL handles OAuth2 token acquisition for Electron main process. FastAPI validates JWT signature against Azure AD JWKS endpoint. RBAC enforced via dependency injection: QualityEngineer (create deviation, submit root cause, implement action), QualityManager (approve CAPAs, sign effectiveness verifications, manage assignments), ProductionSupervisor (read own area deviations, confirm implementation), ReadOnly (view all records). e-signature: meaning text captured at time of signing, SHA-256 record hash stored in esignatures table, printed name of signer, date and time of signing recorded in audit_trail. FDA 21 CFR Part 11: electronic signatures with meaning and date, append-only audit trail, RBAC, MFA. GAMP 5: CSV validation test suite in Validation Master Plan, OQ test scripts drafted during development. NIST SP 800-171: Azure Key Vault for client secrets, Private Endpoint for PostgreSQL.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: Electron NSIS installer distributed via SCCM to facility workstations. NSSM service installed on app server via MSI. Azure DevOps Pipelines: PR gate - pytest 85% coverage, mypy, ruff, Playwright E2E for CAPA workflow happy path. Main merge builds Electron NSIS installer and FastAPI MSI, uploads to Azure Artifacts. Production deployment requires Change Control Board approval and updated validation documentation. IQ/OQ/PQ: CSV validation test suite in Validation Master Plan, OQ test scripts drafted during development, sign-off required from Quality Manager and IT. PostgreSQL backups: daily snapshots to Azure Backup with 35-day retention. No hard delete of any CAPA record; soft delete with status=archived for superseded records only.
parsed.observability: New Relic APM agent on FastAPI Windows service. Custom metrics: CAPA workflow state transitions per day, e-signature capture rate per day, effectiveness verification completion rate. Structured JSON logging with correlation IDs from Electron and FastAPI. Windows Event Log for application errors. Alert: CAPA workflow stuck in any state for more than 24 hours (potential system failure). Alert: e-signature capture rate below 50% for a specific CAPA record (potential user training issue).
parsed.cost_and_scaling: Electron NSIS installer and NSSM MSI on facility app server: no recurring cost. Azure Flexible Server PostgreSQL: ~$150/month for Basic tier. Azure Key Vault: ~$50/month. Azure DevOps Pipelines: ~$200/month. New Relic APM: ~$500/month. Total: ~$900/month. Scaling: PostgreSQL scales horizontally via read replica if CAPA data volume exceeds 100K records.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): Electron shell with NSSM service, deviation entry, root cause analysis with fishbone template, corrective action with due dates, CAPA workflow state machine, Azure AD auth, e-signature capture with SHA-256 record hash, audit_trail, Azure DevOps Pipelines CI/CD, IQ/OQ/PQ CSV validation test suite. Phase 2 (8 weeks): Effectiveness verification scheduling and manual check, CAPA report PDF generation, New Relic APM dashboards, CAPA report export to Excel, Change Control Board approval workflow. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Preventive action module, trend analysis dashboard, integration with MES for automatic deviation creation from out-of-spec inspection results.
parsed.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with strict mypy type checking. ruff for linting. pytest 85% coverage for FastAPI handlers and CAPAWorkflowService. Playwright E2E for all CAPA workflow happy path scenarios. Electron: TypeScript strict mode, ESLint, Prettier. Electron NSSM service must be installed via MSI, not via PowerShell. No hard delete of any CAPA record; all records are soft-deleted with status=archived. All e-signature records must have a valid signer_id FK. CSV validation test suite must be updated before any production deployment.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Electron auto-updates disable validated system configuration. Mitigation: Electron NSIS installer and NSSM MSI installed via Change Control Board approval. Risk: FastAPI Windows service is a non-validated system component and a direct violation of FDA 21 CFR Part 820 computer system validation requirements. Mitigation: NSSM service is the validated system perimeter boundary; Electron shell is the validated system perimeter boundary. Tradeoff: Electron shell over a native desktop application reduces development effort but limits Electron's native access to the file system and clipboard. Tradeoff: PostgreSQL Private Endpoint over a dedicated RDS instance reduces cost but requires a Private Endpoint-compatible database client.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Electron shell chosen over native desktop application to reduce development effort and maintain validated system configuration. Azure AD chosen over on-premises Active Directory for RBAC and MFA. PostgreSQL Private Endpoint chosen over dedicated RDS instance to reduce cost and complexity. CAPA workflow state machine chosen over a BPMN-based workflow to reduce development complexity.

reference.thinking_summary: Designing an FDA 21 CFR Part 11 compliant CAPA desktop application for a medical device manufacturer. This is intentionally a flawed first-pass plan that omits the electronic signature meaning capture and record hash linkage required by 21 CFR Part 11, and underspecifies the effectiveness verification escalation workflow. These gaps are intentional to create a training example for auditor detection of compliance specification failures.
reference.title: Medical Device CAPA Management Desktop Application
reference.executive_summary: An Electron and FastAPI desktop application for quality engineers at a medical device manufacturer managing the full CAPA lifecycle from deviation logging through root cause analysis, corrective action approval, and effectiveness verification. Built on PostgreSQL 16 on Azure with Azure AD authentication, targeting FDA 21 CFR Part 820 and ISO 13485 compliance with computer system validation.
reference.architecture_overview: Electron 31 shell with React 18 renderer communicates over localhost HTTP to a FastAPI Python service running as a Windows service on the facility app server. Azure AD MSAL handles OAuth2 token acquisition in the Electron main process. The FastAPI service connects to PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server via Private Endpoint with Azure AD service account authentication. All state-changing CAPA workflow operations require electronic signature capture. An audit_trail table records every field-level change in the same PostgreSQL transaction as the update. A background effectiveness scheduler checks for overdue verifications on service startup and daily via a scheduled task stored in PostgreSQL.
reference.technology_stack: Frontend: Electron 31, React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Zustand, React Query. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Alembic migrations. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server. Auth: Azure AD MSAL. Distribution: NSIS Electron installer via SCCM, FastAPI NSSM Windows service. CI/CD: Azure DevOps Pipelines. Observability: New Relic APM, structured JSON logging via structlog.
reference.functional_feature_map: Deviation Entry: quality engineer logs deviation with deviation_number (auto-assigned), lot_number, product_code, description, initial severity classification (minor/major/critical). CAPA Initiation: QualityManager creates capa_record linked to deviation, assigns owner and target_closure_date. Root Cause Analysis: assigned engineer selects method (five-why, fishbone, fault-tree), documents root cause; QualityManager approves with e-signature. Corrective Action: engineer creates corrective_action with implementation plan and due date; QualityManager approves. Effectiveness Verification: system schedules verification 90 days post-closure; engineer completes verification form recording outcome (effective/ineffective/inconclusive); QualityManager signs off to close CAPA. CAPA Report: PDF generation showing full lifecycle timeline from deviation to closure.
reference.system_components: Electron 31 main process (Azure AD MSAL, NSSM service health check, IPC bridge), React 18 renderer (CAPA workflow UI, e-signature modal, PDF preview), FastAPI Windows service (CAPA workflow state machine, audit trail writer, effectiveness scheduler, e-signature validator), PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server (deviations, capa_records, root_cause_analyses, corrective_actions, effectiveness_verifications, esignatures, audit_trail, scheduled_tasks), Azure AD tenant (user directory, RBAC group assignments, MFA policy), Azure Private Endpoint (network path from facility app server to PostgreSQL), New Relic APM (FastAPI performance monitoring).
reference.workflows: CAPA Lifecycle: QualityEngineer creates deviation record with lot number and severity; system auto-assigns deviation_number. QualityManager initiates CAPA, assigns to owner. Engineer documents root cause using fishbone template and submits for approval. QualityManager reviews root cause analysis and approves with e-signature (e-signature modal captures meaning text from dropdown: 'Approved as documented'). Engineer creates corrective action with implementation steps and due date; QualityManager approves. Engineer marks implementation complete. System schedules effectiveness verification 90 days later in scheduled_tasks. Effectiveness Check: On scheduled date, system sends reminder notification to assigned engineer. Engineer opens effectiveness form, records outcome and evidence notes, submits. QualityManager reviews and signs e-signature to close CAPA record. If verification outcome is 'ineffective', CAPA status reverts to 'action_planned' and a new action is required.
reference.data_model: deviations: id UUID PK, deviation_number SERIAL UNIQUE, lot_number TEXT, product_code TEXT, detected_by UUID FK, description TEXT, severity ENUM minor/major/critical, status ENUM open/capa_initiated/closed, detected_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ. capa_records: id UUID PK, capa_number SERIAL UNIQUE, deviation_id UUID FK, owner_id UUID FK, status ENUM open/root_cause_assigned/action_planned/in_implementation/effectiveness_check/verification_overdue/closed, target_closure_date DATE, closed_at TIMESTAMPTZ. root_cause_analyses: id UUID PK, capa_id UUID FK, method ENUM five_why/fishbone/fault_tree, root_cause_description TEXT, assigned_to UUID FK, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ. corrective_actions: id UUID PK, capa_id UUID FK, action_description TEXT, assigned_to UUID FK, due_date DATE, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, verification_due_date DATE. effectiveness_verifications: id UUID PK, corrective_action_id UUID FK, scheduled_date DATE, performed_by UUID FK, outcome ENUM effective/ineffective/inconclusive, evidence_notes TEXT, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ. esignatures: id UUID PK, record_type TEXT, record_id UUID, signer_id UUID FK, signed_at TIMESTAMPTZ. audit_trail: id UUID PK, record_type TEXT, record_id UUID, field_name TEXT, old_value TEXT, new_value TEXT, changed_by UUID FK, change_timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ.
reference.api_design: REST at /api/v1. POST /deviations body lot_number product_code description severity returns deviation_id and deviation_number. GET /deviations query params status severity product_code returns paginated list. POST /capa-records body deviation_id owner_id target_closure_date returns capa_id. PATCH /capa-records/:id/root-cause body root_cause_description method esig_payload transitions to root_cause_assigned. PATCH /capa-records/:id/approve-root-cause body esig_payload QualityManager role required. POST /corrective-actions body capa_id action_description due_date. PATCH /corrective-actions/:id/complete body esig_payload. POST /effectiveness-verifications body corrective_action_id outcome evidence_notes. PATCH /effectiveness-verifications/:id/approve body esig_payload closes CAPA. GET /capa-records/:id/report generates PDF. GET /audit-trail/:record_type/:record_id returns change history.
reference.security_and_compliance: Azure AD MSAL with MFA for all user roles. RBAC enforced in FastAPI via dependency injection checking Azure AD group membership. TLS 1.3 for all client-to-service communication. Audit trail written append-only in same PostgreSQL transaction as every record change; PostgreSQL row-level trigger prevents UPDATE and DELETE on audit_trail rows. Electronic signatures capture signer Azure AD identity and timestamp. FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requires signature meaning text - captured via dropdown (options: 'Approved as documented', 'Reviewed and accepted', 'Implemented as described'). Record hash linkage at time of signature is not implemented in this version and represents a known compliance gap requiring resolution before IQ/OQ/PQ. 15-year record retention enforced via Azure Backup policy.
reference.deployment_and_operations: FastAPI packaged as MSI installer configuring NSSM Windows service on facility app server. Electron packaged as NSIS installer distributed via SCCM to quality department workstations. Azure DevOps Pipelines: PR gate runs pytest 85% coverage, mypy, ruff, Playwright E2E tests against dev database. Build pipeline produces versioned MSI and NSIS artifacts uploaded to Azure Artifacts. Production deployment requires Change Control Board approval, updated Validation Master Plan, and execution of affected OQ test scripts. Three environments: dev (Azure Flexible Server dev tier), validation (identical to production for IQ/OQ/PQ execution), production. Alembic database migrations applied during MSI install via pre-install script.
reference.observability: New Relic APM agent on FastAPI service: transaction traces for CAPA workflow state transitions, error rate dashboard, CAPA approval latency p95. Structured JSON logging via structlog to Windows Event Log and log file. Custom metrics: open_deviations_by_severity gauge, overdue_effectiveness_verifications count, capa_cycle_time_days histogram. Alert: overdue_effectiveness_verifications > 0 triggers email to QualityManager and logs to Windows Event Log as Warning level for IT monitoring.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Azure Flexible Server Burstable B2ms tier: approximately $120/month. Azure Backup: approximately $30/month for 15-year retention policy with daily snapshots. Azure Private Endpoint: approximately $10/month. Total infrastructure approximately $160/month. Scales to 500 concurrent CAPA records comfortably on Burstable tier; upgrade to General Purpose for larger facilities with 2000+ active records.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 weeks 1-8: FastAPI service with deviation and CAPA record modules, PostgreSQL schema with Alembic migrations, Azure AD auth, audit trail, e-signature capture without record hash (known gap), Electron shell with React UI for deviation entry and CAPA workflow, Azure DevOps CI/CD, IQ documentation. Phase 2 weeks 9-16: Root cause analysis module with fishbone template, corrective action module, effectiveness verification scheduler, CAPA report PDF generation, OQ test script execution with formal sign-off. Phase 3 weeks 17-24: E-signature record hash linkage (closes 21 CFR Part 11 gap), effectiveness escalation workflow, PQ execution and CSV sign-off, SCCM distribution to production workstations.
reference.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 strict typing with mypy. SQLAlchemy 2.0 async session management with explicit transaction boundaries for audit trail writes. All CAPA workflow state transitions tested via pytest with state machine assertion helpers. Electron renderer TypeScript strict mode. Each release requires updated traceability matrix linking requirements to test cases for CSV. No production deployment without Change Control Board sign-off.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: e-signature record hash linkage missing in Phase 1 creates a known 21 CFR Part 11 gap that must be resolved before PQ sign-off. This is an intentional phasing decision documented in the Validation Master Plan. Risk: effectiveness verification scheduler missed deadlines if Windows service is stopped for maintenance. Mitigation: scheduler catch-up on service restart processes all overdue scheduled_tasks; escalation email sent for any task more than 24 hours overdue. Tradeoff: NSSM Windows service over Docker for facility IT compatibility with existing Windows server infrastructure - avoids Docker licensing cost and IT retraining. Tradeoff: PostgreSQL on Azure over on-premises SQL Server to leverage managed backup and high availability without facility DBA overhead.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Electronic signature record hash: deferred to Phase 3 with documented gap in Validation Master Plan. Effectiveness scheduler catch-up: processes all overdue scheduled_tasks on service restart. SCCM packaging: NSIS installer for Electron, MSI for FastAPI service, both signed with facility code signing certificate.

{
 "thinking_summary": "This is a partner-facing API service migrating from a monolith farm management system. The key architectural decisions are: PostGIS ST_AsGeoJSON for field boundary serialization, OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow with PKCE for equipment terminals, consent records as the database-layer access control layer rather than JWT scope claims, and a zero-downtime parallel deployment window of 4 weeks to allow OEM partners to verify the migrated endpoint before the monolith endpoint is deprecated.",
 "fix_report": [],

parsed.thinking_summary: This is a partner-facing API service migrating from a monolith farm management system. The key architectural decisions are: PostGIS ST_AsGeoJSON for field boundary serialization, OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow with PKCE for equipment terminals, consent records as the database-layer access control layer rather than JWT scope claims, and a zero-downtime parallel deployment window of 4 weeks to allow OEM partners to verify the migrated endpoint before the monolith endpoint is deprecated.
parsed.title: Farm Field Boundary and Prescription Management API
parsed.executive_summary: A partner-facing REST API service on AWS ECS Fargate that exposes farm field boundaries and variable-rate prescription maps to authorized precision agriculture equipment OEM terminals and farm software tools via OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow. Built with Node.js 20 Fastify and PostGIS on RDS PostgreSQL 16, the service enforces consent-based access control at the database query layer and returns GeoJSON Feature Collections for field boundaries and JSON with zones array for prescription maps. A zero-downtime parallel deployment window of 4 weeks allows OEM partners to verify the migrated endpoint before the monolith endpoint is deprecated.
parsed.architecture_overview: Single-service architecture on ECS Fargate with three route domains: /auth (OAuth 2.0 authorization server), /v1/fields (boundary queries), /v1/prescriptions (map CRUD and download). ALB routes /auth/* to oauth-service and /v1/* to api-service. TLS 1.3 enforced at ALB. JWTs validated at ALB and Fastify middleware. Consent records stored in PostgreSQL as the database-layer access control layer, not JWT scope claims. PostGIS RDS stores field_boundaries and prescription_maps with GiST indexes. Redis 7 caches OAuth JWTs with TTL matching JWT exp claim. WAF rules block SQL injection and rate-limit per source IP.
parsed.technology_stack: Runtime: Node.js 20. Framework: Fastify with JSON Schema validation. Spatial: node-postgres with PostGIS 1.11 for field boundary serialization. Auth: node-oauth2-server with custom adapter for PostgreSQL token storage. Cache: Redis 7 for OAuth JWT cache and rate limit counters. Database: RDS PostgreSQL 16 with GiST index on field_boundaries.geometry. Auth: custom OAuth 2.0 authorization server with RS256 JWT issuance. Hosting: ECS Fargate, ALB with WAF, S3 for ISOXML export. CI: GitHub Actions. Monitoring: AWS CloudWatch, PagerDuty.
parsed.functional_feature_map: Field Boundary Endpoint: GET /v1/fields/{field_id}/boundary returns GeoJSON Feature Collection with field_id, field_name, acreage, crop_type, geometry. Prescription Map Endpoint: GET /v1/prescriptions/{field_id}/map returns JSON with product_name, rate_units, zones array (compatible with monolith schema). Consent Management: POST /v1/consent/grant creates consent record for equipment terminal. API Key Management: POST /v1/keys/create generates API key for server-to-server integrations. Webhook Notifications: POST /v1/webhooks/subscribe registers webhook endpoint for prescription updates. Audit Log: GET /v1/audit/log returns access audit trail for partner support.
parsed.system_components: ALB with WAF rules (SQL injection and rate-limit blocking), ECS Fargate api-service (Fastify app, 2-8 tasks, TLS termination), ECS Fargate oauth-service (OAuth 2.0 endpoints, 2 tasks), RDS PostgreSQL 16 with GiST index on field_boundaries.geometry, Redis 7 for OAuth JWT cache and rate limit counters, S3 for ISOXML prescription map exports, AWS Secrets Manager for credentials and RS256 private key, CloudWatch for ECS metrics and API access logs.
parsed.workflows: Field Boundary Fetch: Equipment terminal sends POST /v1/fields/{field_id}/boundary with Authorization: Bearer {JWT} and X-Client-ID: {client_id}; ALB validates JWT and WAF rules; Fastify middleware checks consent scope (fields:read); PostGIS query returns ST_AsGeoJSON for field_boundaries table; response returned as GeoJSON Feature Collection with geometry and properties; WAF logs access event to CloudWatch. Prescription Map Download: Server-to-server integration sends POST /v1/prescriptions/{field_id}/map with Authorization: Bearer {API Key}; ALB validates API key; Fastify middleware checks consent scope (prescriptions:read); PostGIS query returns prescription_maps with zones array; response returned as JSON with product_name and zones array; WAF logs access event to CloudWatch.
parsed.data_model: field_boundaries (id UUID PK, operator_account_id UUID FK, field_name VARCHAR, acreage NUMERIC, crop_type VARCHAR, geometry GEOMETRY(MULTIPOLYGON,4326), created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, version INT). prescription_maps (id UUID PK, field_id UUID FK, operator_account_id UUID FK, product_name VARCHAR, rate_units VARCHAR, zones_json JSONB, published_at TIMESTAMPTZ, superseded_by_id UUID nullable). data_sharing_consents (id UUID PK, operator_account_id UUID FK, grantee_client_id UUID FK, scope_granted TEXT[], granted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, revoked_at TIMESTAMPTZ, revocation_reason VARCHAR). api_access_grants (id UUID PK, client_id UUID FK, key_hash VARCHAR, quota_per_minute INT, last_rotated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, revoked_at TIMESTAMPTZ). api_audit_log (id BIGSERIAL PK, client_id UUID, operator_account_id UUID, endpoint VARCHAR, method VARCHAR, status_code INT, latency_ms INT, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ).
parsed.api_design: POST /v1/consent/grant body: {operator_account_id, grantee_client_id, scope_granted}. Returns {consent_id, granted_at}. GET /v1/consent/revoke body: {consent_id}. Returns {success: true}. GET /v1/fields/{field_id}/boundary returns GeoJSON Feature Collection with geometry and properties. GET /v1/prescriptions/{field_id}/map returns JSON with product_name and zones array. POST /v1/prescriptions/{field_id}/map body: {product_name, rate_units, zones_json}. PATCH /v1/prescriptions/{id}/publish body: {published_at}. GET /v1/audit/log?operator_account_id=&from=&to= returns access audit trail. POST /v1/webhooks/subscribe body: {endpoint} returns {webhook_id}. All endpoints require Authorization: Bearer {JWT} and X-Client-ID: {client_id}; X-Client-ID must match the client_id in the consent record for the requested scope.
parsed.security_and_compliance: OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow with PKCE for equipment terminals. Client credentials flow for server-to-server integrations. RS256 JWT with scope claims (fields:read, prescriptions:read, prescriptions:write) and operator_account_id embedded. Consent records as database-layer access control; JWT scope claims never grant access beyond what the consent record explicitly permits. Consent records are hard-marked as revoked_at in DB and invalidate Redis cached tokens immediately on revocation. API key SHA-256 hash stored in DB; 90-day rotation reminder via CloudWatch notification. WAF rules block SQL injection patterns and rate-limit per source IP. TLS 1.3 enforced at ALB. AWS Secrets Manager for credentials and RS256 private key. Data-sharing audit log retained 90 days for AgGateway compliance.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: lint, jest unit tests (90% coverage gate), OpenAPI spec validation against response fixtures, Docker build, push ECR, ECS rolling deploy. Staging environment runs OEM partner integration smoke tests against real terminal emulators before production promotion. Parallel deployment window of 4 weeks: monolith endpoint remains live during parallel deployment; OEM partners verify migrated endpoint before monolith endpoint is deprecated. ALB connection draining set to 30s for zero-downtime. Environments: dev (single-AZ, shared RDS dev instance), staging (multi-AZ, shared RDS dev instance), production (multi-AZ, RDS Multi-AZ, auto-scaling on ALB request count metric).
parsed.observability: AWS CloudWatch metrics: ECS task count, ALB request count, API error rate (5xx), OAuth token cache hit rate. Custom CloudWatch metrics: consent grant rate per partner, API error rate per endpoint, prescription download count.
