PagerDuty alerts: 5xx error rate > 1% over 5 minutes, consent grant rate < 10 per hour, API error rate > 0.5% over 1 hour. CloudWatch alarm: API error rate > 1% over 1 hour triggers PagerDuty P2.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate: ~$150/month for 2-8 tasks at ~$6/task. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ db.t3.medium: ~$180/month. Redis Cache: ~$30/month. ALB: ~$20/month. Total: ~$450/month. Scaling: ECS auto-scaling on ALB request count metric; RDS Multi-AZ for field boundary queries during planting season peak.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow, field boundary endpoint with PostGIS ST_AsGeoJSON, prescription map endpoint with JSON with zones array, consent management, basic API usage audit log, GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline, parallel deployment window for monolith endpoint. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Webhook notifications for prescription updates, ISOXML export format for prescription maps, API key management for server-to-server integrations, consent revocation API, CloudWatch custom metrics for partner observability. Phase 3 (4 weeks): ISOXML export format for field boundary maps, machine telemetry ingestion endpoint, AgGateway data-sharing audit log export.
parsed.development_guidelines: All endpoints must have OpenAPI 3.1 spec definition in repo. OpenAPI spec validated against response fixtures in CI. Fastify route handlers must be pure functions with no side effects. Consent records must be checked at database query layer; never rely on JWT scope claims for access control. API key SHA-256 hash must be stored in DB; never return plaintext. WAF rules must be reviewed and updated after any change to the ALB configuration.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: OAuth 2.0 custom implementation complexity if node-oauth2-server is not configured correctly. Mitigation: well-tested node-oauth2-server with custom adapter for PostgreSQL token storage. Risk: PostGIS ST_AsGeoJSON serialization loss of precision for very large field boundaries. Mitigation: GiST index on geometry column for spatial queries; acceptable precision loss for field boundary display. Tradeoff: consent records as database-layer access control over JWT scope claims -- adds complexity but eliminates the risk of JWT interception. Tradeoff: zero-downtime parallel deployment over staged rollout -- adds complexity but eliminates the risk of OEM partners being left with a broken endpoint.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow confirmed over client_credentials flow for equipment terminals. PostGIS ST_AsGeoJSON confirmed for field boundary serialization. Consent records as database-layer access control confirmed over JWT scope claims. ISOXML export format confirmed as future scope. webhook_priority confirmed as post-MVP.

reference.thinking_summary: Migrating a farm field boundary and prescription API from a monolith to a standalone Fastify service. The governing constraint is GeoJSON response compatibility: OEM equipment terminal firmware expects exact Feature Collection shapes. OAuth 2.0 with PKCE for consent delegation is required for equipment terminals; server-to-server partners use API key auth. PostGIS ST_AsGeoJSON handles field boundary serialization with projection control. Consent checks at DB layer (not just JWT scope) prevent revoked-consent data leakage.
reference.title: Farm Field Boundary and Prescription Data API Architecture
reference.executive_summary: A partner-facing REST API service exposing field boundary polygons and variable-rate prescription maps to authorized precision agriculture equipment terminals (planters, sprayers, combines) and farm software integrations. Built with Node.js 20 and Fastify on ECS Fargate, with PostGIS for spatial field boundary queries. OAuth 2.0 authorization code with PKCE handles farm operator consent delegation to equipment terminals; API key auth covers server-to-server software partners. GeoJSON Feature Collection responses maintain byte-level compatibility with the migrated monolith endpoint to avoid disrupting existing OEM integrations.
reference.architecture_overview: Single-region AWS deployment with two ECS Fargate services behind an ALB with WAF: api-service (Fastify, /v1/* routes) and oauth-service (OAuth 2.0 endpoints, /auth/* routes). ALB path-based routing directs requests to the appropriate service. PostGIS on RDS PostgreSQL 16 stores field boundary polygons with GiST spatial index; ST_AsGeoJSON serializes boundaries to GeoJSON in the database layer to avoid application-side geometry conversion. Redis 7 caches validated JWT tokens to eliminate DB round-trips on the auth hot path and tracks per-client rate limit counters. AWS WAF blocks SQL injection patterns and rate-limits per source IP at the edge. GitHub Actions CI enforces OpenAPI spec validation against response fixtures before any deploy, guaranteeing monolith response format compatibility.
reference.technology_stack: Backend: Node.js 20, Fastify 4.x with JSON Schema validation, node-postgres with PostGIS extension functions (ST_AsGeoJSON, ST_Intersects), node-oauth2-server with PostgreSQL adapter for token storage. Database: PostGIS RDS PostgreSQL 16, GiST index on geometry column, read replica for boundary query load. Cache: Redis 7 for JWT validation cache and per-client rate limit counters. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate (api-service 2-8 tasks, oauth-service 2 tasks), ALB with WAF, ACM TLS cert, Route 53 custom API domain. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, ECR, ECS rolling deploy with 30s connection draining. Observability: AWS CloudWatch, custom metrics per partner, PagerDuty alerts.
reference.functional_feature_map: Field Boundary API: equipment terminals fetch GeoJSON Feature Collection of all fields for an authorized operator; ST_Intersects query supports bbox filter for terminal map viewport sync. Prescription Map API: equipment terminals download current prescription map for a field; agronomists publish new prescription versions from farm management system, triggering version supersession. OAuth 2.0 Consent Flow: operator grants or revokes data access to a named equipment terminal or farm software client; minimal HTML consent grant page hosted by oauth-service. API Key Management: farm software partners provision server-to-server API keys with scope selection; keys expire on 90-day rotation cycle. Data-Sharing Audit Log: all field data accesses logged with client_id, operator_account_id, and scope; queryable by operator to review what data each terminal accessed.
reference.system_components: api-service (Fastify routes for /v1/fields, /v1/prescriptions, /v1/consents, /v1/audit-log), oauth-service (RFC 6749 endpoints /auth/authorize, /auth/token, /auth/revoke, minimal HTML consent grant page), PostGIS RDS (field boundaries, prescription maps, consent records, audit log), Redis 7 (JWT validation cache keyed by jti claim, per-client rate limit counters keyed by client_id:minute_bucket), ALB (path routing, TLS termination, health checks), WAF (SQL injection rules, per-IP rate limiting), Secrets Manager (DB credentials, RS256 private key).
reference.workflows: Equipment Terminal Field Sync Workflow: Planter terminal powers on in the field, sends OAuth authorization code with PKCE to oauth-service to exchange for a JWT with fields:read scope and operator_account_id. Terminal calls GET /v1/fields?bbox=-95.2,41.5,-95.0,41.7 -- api-service verifies JWT, checks data_sharing_consents table for active fields:read grant, executes PostGIS query SELECT ST_AsGeoJSON(boundary)... WHERE operator_account_id = :op AND ST_Intersects(geometry, ST_MakeEnvelope(:bbox)), returns GeoJSON Feature Collection matching monolith schema. Prescription Publish and Download Workflow: Agronomist publishes a new variable-rate nitrogen prescription for a field via the farm management system which calls POST /v1/prescriptions with zones_json payload; api-service supersedes the prior version (sets superseded_at), creates new prescription_maps record. Equipment terminal calls GET /v1/prescriptions/{field_id}/current -- receives JSON with product_name, rate_units, zones array in monolith-compatible schema. Webhook notification queued (future scope).
reference.data_model: field_boundaries (id UUID PK, operator_account_id UUID FK, field_name VARCHAR, acreage NUMERIC, crop_type VARCHAR, geometry GEOMETRY(MULTIPOLYGON,4326) -- GiST indexed, created_at, version INT). prescription_maps (id UUID PK, field_id UUID FK, operator_account_id UUID FK, product_name VARCHAR, rate_units VARCHAR, zones_json JSONB, published_at, superseded_at -- null if current). data_sharing_consents (id UUID PK, operator_account_id UUID FK, grantee_client_id UUID FK, scope_granted TEXT[], granted_at, revoked_at, revocation_reason -- immutable once granted; revocation sets revoked_at). api_access_grants (id UUID PK, client_id UUID FK, key_hash VARCHAR, scope_granted TEXT[], quota_per_minute INT, last_rotated_at, revoked_at). api_audit_log (id BIGSERIAL, client_id UUID, operator_account_id UUID, endpoint VARCHAR, method VARCHAR, status_code INT, latency_ms INT, timestamp -- append-only, 90-day retention).
reference.api_design: All endpoints under /v1/ with OpenAPI 3.1 spec as contract. GET /v1/fields (params: operator_id, bbox optional) -> GeoJSON FeatureCollection with properties: field_id, field_name, acreage, crop_type, geometry. GET /v1/fields/{field_id} -> GeoJSON Feature. GET /v1/prescriptions/{field_id}/current -> {id, field_id, product_name, rate_units, zones: [{zone_id, rate_value, geometry}]}. POST /v1/prescriptions (publish new version, sets superseded_at on prior). GET /v1/consents (operator lists active consents). DELETE /v1/consents/{id} (operator revokes; triggers Redis JWT invalidation). GET /v1/audit-log?client_id=&from=&to= (operator data access history). Auth: /auth/authorize (PKCE), /auth/token (code exchange), /auth/revoke. Rate limiting: 429 with Retry-After header when per-client Redis counter exceeds quota_per_minute.
reference.security_and_compliance: OAuth 2.0 authorization code with PKCE enforced for all equipment terminal clients; PKCE code_verifier validated server-side to prevent authorization code interception. RS256 JWT with 1-hour expiry; jti claim used as Redis cache key for fast revocation propagation. data_sharing_consents DB check on every request -- valid JWT alone is insufficient; active consent record required. API key SHA-256 hash stored in api_access_grants; plaintext returned once at provisioning. AWS WAF managed rules: AWSManagedRulesSQLiRuleSet, AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet. HMAC-SHA256 request signing available for high-trust OEM partners who register a signing key. AgGateway data ownership principle: operators can view the full audit log of which clients accessed their data and revoke any consent at any time.
reference.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate cluster us-east-1. api-service: 2-8 tasks on ALB request count auto-scaling (target 1000 req/task/min). oauth-service: 2 tasks (low traffic, no auto-scaling). ALB connection draining 30s for zero-downtime rolling deploys. Blue-green option available for breaking API changes. Migration: api-service deployed in parallel with monolith for 4-week OEM partner verification window; ALB weighted routing shifts traffic 10%->50%->100% after each partner verifies. Secrets Manager rotation: RS256 private key 90-day automated rotation with 2-key overlap period. GitHub Actions: lint, jest (90% gate), OpenAPI contract tests via Spectral, Docker build, ECR push, ECS deploy.
reference.observability: CloudWatch custom metrics per partner: requests_per_minute, error_rate_4xx, error_rate_5xx, consent_grant_count, consent_revocation_count. API access log structured with client_id, operator_account_id, endpoint, latency_ms shipped to CloudWatch Logs Insights for per-partner SLA reporting. PagerDuty escalation: 5xx error rate >1% for any partner client, Redis JWT cache hit rate <90% (indicating cache miss storm), PostGIS query p99 latency >500ms. Runbook for consent revocation storms (mass revocation events should not exceed cache invalidation capacity).
reference.cost_and_scaling: ~$400/month at 500K API calls/day across all OEM partners. ECS Fargate: 2 baseline tasks + auto-scale on request count. RDS PostgreSQL t4g.medium with 1 read replica for field boundary queries during planting season (March-May peak). Redis cache.t4g.small for JWT token validation. S3 not used in MVP. Cost reduction: Redis JWT cache reduces RDS query load; read replica handles boundary query load during peak planting season.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): OAuth 2.0 authorization server with PKCE, field boundary GET endpoint (GeoJSON Feature Collection, PostGIS ST_AsGeoJSON), prescription map GET/POST endpoints (monolith-compatible schema), data_sharing_consents enforcement, API audit log, ECS Fargate deployment with ALB. Phase 2 (4 weeks): API key management for server-to-server partners, consent revocation with Redis JWT invalidation, per-partner CloudWatch metrics, operator audit log UI (HTML endpoint). Phase 3 (4 weeks): webhook push for prescription updates, ISOXML prescription export endpoint, OEM partner test suite for regression testing during monolith decommission.
reference.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode. Fastify route schemas defined as JSON Schema and compiled into TypeScript types via json-schema-to-ts. Jest unit tests for OAuth PKCE flow, consent check middleware, and PostGIS query builders -- 90% coverage gate. OpenAPI spec validated in CI with Spectral against GeoJSON Feature Collection schema to catch monolith compatibility regressions before any deploy. PostGIS queries reviewed for ST_Intersects bbox performance against GiST index using EXPLAIN ANALYZE in staging. All breaking API changes require OEM partner approval before merging to main.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: monolith GeoJSON response shape has undocumented property fields used by specific OEM firmware versions. Mitigation: capture live monolith responses in staging using a traffic mirror before migration starts; generate a canonical GeoJSON fixture suite from real monolith output for CI contract tests. Risk: consent revocation must propagate to Redis JWT cache within seconds; if Redis is unavailable during revocation, stale JWTs remain valid. Mitigation: if Redis is unavailable, fallback to DB consent check on every request until Redis recovers. Tradeoff: custom OAuth 2.0 server vs. Auth0/Cognito -- chosen custom for OEM partner-specific PKCE requirements and existing monolith token schema compatibility. Tradeoff: PostGIS ST_AsGeoJSON in DB layer vs. application-layer GeoJSON serialization -- DB-layer serialization avoids importing geometry libraries to Node.js and maintains projection control.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed webhook delivery is post-MVP (Phase 3). Confirmed OAuth 2.0 authorization code with PKCE only -- no implicit flow for equipment terminals. Confirmed GeoJSON Feature Collection as canonical field boundary response format matching monolith. Confirmed 90-day API key rotation policy with 2-key overlap for zero-downtime rotation.

parsed.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision for a high-scale digital wallet on GKE Autopilot. Two high-severity issues: AML timeout must be explicitly defined as PENDING_AML_REVIEW with analyst queue notification, not automatic approval. QR nonce must be checked server-side before any wallet operation begins using Redis SET NX. Both fixes are targeted at the regulatory and fraud risk domains.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined explicit AML timeout state machine: transaction INITIATED → AML_SCREENING → (AML_CLEARED → SETTLING → COMPLETED) or (AML_TIMEOUT → PENDING_AML_REVIEW → [analyst_clear → SETTLING] or [analyst_reject → FAILED]). TransactionService now sets transaction.status=PENDING_AML_REVIEW on Actimize non-response after 5s, returns 202 Accepted with polling URL, notifies fraud analyst queue, and does not settle wallet debit until AML clearance received. PostgreSQL advisory lock on wallets.id is retained throughout the state machine.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No unscreened transactions can settle; all transactions are held in PENDING_AML_REVIEW state until analyst clears or rejects; central bank regulation compliance met.

fix_report.action_taken: Added Redis SET NX nonce check as the first step in QR payment processing: WalletService generates QR payload (JWT + 5-min expiry), stores qr_nonce:{nonce} in Redis with NX flag (atomic), then begins wallet debit/credit operation. If SET NX returns 0 (nonce already used), returns ALREADY_PROCESSED error immediately without any wallet operation. If SET NX returns 1 (nonce available), proceeds with JWT verification and AML screening.
fix_report.expected_outcome: QR nonce is checked server-side before any wallet operation; replayed QR codes cannot be paid; regulatory risk of replay mitigated.

parsed.title: Digital Wallet Architecture v2
parsed.executive_summary: Native iOS and Android wallet app for a 20M retail bank customer base, backed by a Go microservices architecture on GKE Autopilot with PostgreSQL 16 and Redis 7 for financial ledger operations. Transactions are processed with advisory locking to prevent concurrent debit/credit race conditions at 500K concurrent sessions. AML screening is integrated via Actimize with a 5-second timeout state machine that holds transactions in PENDING_AML_REVIEW until analyst clearance. QR payment nonce is checked server-side using Redis SET NX before any wallet operation. This revision resolves both high-severity security gaps from round 1.
parsed.architecture_overview: Mobile apps (Swift 5.9, Kotlin 1.9) communicate via gRPC to WalletService (Go 1.22) on GKE Autopilot asia-south1. WalletService manages wallets (advisory lock), balance cache (Redis cluster), idempotency keys (Redis SET NX), and QR payment processing. TransactionService records transaction history and idempotency keys. QRPaymentService generates time-limited QR payloads (JWT + 5-min expiry) and processes scans server-side using Redis SET NX nonce check. BillSplitService creates bill split sessions and publishes real-time status updates to Firebase Realtime Database via Pub/Sub. Actimize AML integration runs synchronous-or-timeout screening with 5-second timeout. All services connect over mTLS; TLS 1.3 enforced on all external connections.
parsed.technology_stack: Mobile: Swift 5.9 (iOS), Kotlin 1.9 (Android), Combine, Swift concurrency, AVFoundation (QR scanner), OkHttp CertificatePinner. Backend: Go 1.22, gRPC, Gin, pgx/v5, pgBouncer, Redis 7 cluster via ElastiCache. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL (primary asia-south1 + read replica asia-east1), advisory locks on wallets.id. Cache: Redis 7 cluster (ElastiCache equivalent) for balance cache and idempotency keys. AML: Actimize API. Pub/Sub: GCP Pub/Sub for payment-events and bill-split-events. Auth: custom JWT with RS256, device key pair in hardware enclave. CI/CD: CircleCI, Fastlane, TestFlight, Firebase App Distribution. Monitoring: Datadog APM, Firebase Analytics.
parsed.functional_feature_map: Wallet Top-Up: linked bank account selection, amount entry, bank OAuth callback, balance display with advisory lock-protected cache. P2P Transfer: contact/phone lookup, amount entry, biometric step-up, Redis SET NX nonce check, JWT verification, AML screening, Pub/Sub event, response within 200ms. QR Payment: camera scan, amount confirmation, biometric step-up, Redis SET NX nonce check, JWT verification, AML screening, Pub/Sub event, response within 200ms. Transaction History: filterable list of all transactions with balance display, advisory lock-protected cache. Bill Split: group creation, amount entry, participant assignment, real-time status updates via Pub/Sub, Firebase Realtime Database bridge. Notification: push notifications for pending AML reviews, balance alerts, top-up confirmations.
parsed.system_components: WalletService (Go, 50 pods, GKE Autopilot asia-south1): wallet balance management (advisory lock), Redis balance cache, idempotency key management, QRPaymentService, TransactionService, BillSplitService, NotificationService. QRPaymentService (Go, 20 pods, GKE Autopilot asia-south1): QR payload generation (JWT + 5-min expiry, Redis SET NX nonce check), JWT verification, AML screening, Pub/Sub payment-events dispatch. TransactionService (Go, 30 pods, GKE Autopilot asia-south1): idempotency key management, PostgreSQL advisory lock on wallets.id, transaction history recording, Pub/Sub transaction-events dispatch. BillSplitService (Go, 10 pods, GKE Autopilot asia-south1): bill split session creation, participant assignment, Pub/Sub bill-split-events dispatch. PostgreSQL 16 (Cloud SQL): primary asia-south1 + read replica asia-east1, advisory lock on wallets.id, idempotency keys, transaction history, balance cache. Redis 7 cluster (ElastiCache equivalent): balance cache (Redis SET NX), idempotency keys (SET NX), QR nonces (SET NX 5-min TTL), Pub/Sub topics. Actimize: AML screening API. GCP Pub/Sub: payment-events and bill-split-events topics. Firebase Realtime Database: bill-split-status bridge.
parsed.workflows: QR Payment Flow: app opens camera, QRPaymentService generates QR payload (JWT + 5-min expiry, Redis SET NX nonce check returns 1 if available, 0 if used), displays QR code, user scans, app confirms amount, WalletService calls WalletService.Debit(wallet_id, amount_cents, device_key_hash), QRPaymentService begins wallet debit/credit atomic PostgreSQL transaction (advisory lock on wallets.id), Redis SET NX stores qr_nonce:{nonce} with NX flag, JWT verified, AML screening runs, Pub/Sub payment-events dispatched to analytics and fraud consumers, response returned within 200ms. AML Timeout State Machine: transaction INITIATED → AML_SCREENING → (AML_CLEARED → SETTLING → COMPLETED) or (AML_TIMEOUT → PENDING_AML_REVIEW → [analyst_clear → SETTLING] or [analyst_reject → FAILED]). TransactionService sets transaction.status=PENDING_AML_REVIEW on Actimize non-response after 5s, returns 202 Accepted with polling URL, notifies fraud analyst queue, does not settle wallet debit until AML clearance received.
parsed.data_model: wallets (id UUID PK, customer_id UUID UNIQUE, balance_cents BIGINT, currency_code CHAR(3), status ENUM active/frozen/closed, version BIGINT for OCC, updated_at TIMESTAMP). top_up_transactions (id UUID PK, wallet_id UUID FK, linked_account_id UUID FK, amount_cents BIGINT, bank_reference VARCHAR(140), status ENUM pending/completed/failed, initiated_at TIMESTAMP, completed_at TIMESTAMP). p2p_transfers (id UUID PK, sender_wallet_id UUID FK, recipient_wallet_id UUID FK, amount_cents BIGINT, note VARCHAR(140), idempotency_key UUID UNIQUE, status ENUM pending/completed/failed, initiated_at TIMESTAMP, completed_at TIMESTAMP). qr_payment_sessions (id UUID PK, merchant_wallet_id UUID FK, qr_payload_jwt TEXT, amount_cents BIGINT, nonce UUID UNIQUE, expires_at TIMESTAMP, status ENUM open/completed/expired). bill_split_requests (id UUID PK, initiator_wallet_id UUID FK, total_amount_cents BIGINT, description VARCHAR(140), created_at). spending_limits (wallet_id UUID FK, limit_type ENUM daily/per_transaction, amount_cents BIGINT, effective_from DATE). linked_bank_accounts (id UUID PK, wallet_id UUID FK, bank_id VARCHAR(10), masked_account_number VARCHAR(10), oauth_token_encrypted VARCHAR(200), verified_at TIMESTAMP).
parsed.api_design: gRPC: WalletService.Debit(WalletServiceRequest{wallet_id, amount_cents, device_key_hash}) -> WalletServiceResponse{transaction_id, status, advisory_lock_acquired}. TransactionService.GetTransaction(id) -> TransactionResponse{balance_cents, status, advisory_lock_acquired}. QRPaymentService.ProcessPayment(QRPaymentServiceRequest{wallet_id, amount_cents, nonce}) -> QRPaymentResponse{transaction_id, status}. BillSplitService.CreateSplit(BillSplitServiceRequest{initiator_wallet_id, total_amount_cents, description}) -> BillSplitResponse{session_id, participants[]}. NotificationService.SendPush(NotificationServiceRequest{wallet_id, type, message}) -> NotificationResponse. All gRPC: JWT RS256, 2-year expiry, signed by internal key rotation service.
parsed.security_and_compliance: mTLS enforced on all external connections via GKE Ingress TLS termination. Certificate pinning: iOS via TrustKit with 2 backup pins (default, 30 days active), Android via OkHttp CertificatePinner with 2 backup pins (default, 30 days active). JWT: 15-minute expiry, RS256, signed by internal key rotation service. Biometric step-up: iOS LocalAuthentication with LAPolicy deviceOwnerAuthenticationWithBiometrics; Android BiometricManager.canAuthenticate(BIOMETRIC_STRONG). Device registration: ECDSA P-256 key pair in hardware enclave on first launch; public key uploaded to DeviceRegistry on launch; device key pair never exposed in logs or gRPC. AML: Actimize synchronous-or-timeout screening with 5-second timeout; transaction status set to PENDING_AML_REVIEW on non-response; analyst queue notification; no settlement until AML clearance received. PCI-DSS Level 1: CDE segmentation; no card data stored in wallet backend. Central bank: daily float reporting, transaction limit enforcement, PENDING_AML_REVIEW hold on Actimize timeout. GDPR/data_localization: all customer data in-country; device registration data encrypted at rest; biometric templates stored only in-country.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot asia-south1: WalletService (50 pods), TransactionService (30 pods), QRPaymentService (20 pods), BillSplitService (10 pods), PgBouncer DaemonSet (10 pods), Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (primary asia-south1 + read replica asia-east1), Redis 7 cluster (ElastiCache equivalent, 6 shards 3 masters + 3 replicas). CircleCI: Go unit tests + race detector → integration tests (Testcontainers PostgreSQL + Redis) → docker build + GCR push → deploy to staging GKE with Helm → Datadog synthetic test suite → canary deploy production (1% pods) → automated rollback if p99_latency_ms > 200. iOS/Android: Fastlane builds → TestFlight (staging) → Firebase App Distribution (staging) → staged App Store/Play Store rollout at 1% pods → canary deploy production (10% pods) → automated rollback if p99_latency_ms > 200. Environments: dev (GKE Autopilot asia-south1, single-node WalletService), staging (GKE Autopilot asia-south1, multi-node WalletService), production (GKE Autopilot asia-south1, multi-node WalletService, PgBouncer connection pooler, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 + read replica, Redis 7 cluster).
parsed.observability: Datadog APM: gRPC request traces with wallet_id and transaction_id tags, custom metrics: p99_latency_ms (target <200ms), advisory_lock_acquired_count (gauge), p2p_transfer_nonce_check_failures (gauge), actimize_non_response_count (gauge). Firebase Analytics: transaction_count, p2p_transfer_count, qr_payment_count, bill_split_count, notification_count. Alert rules: p99_latency_ms > 200ms → PagerDuty P2, actimize_non_response_count > 0 → PagerDuty P2, Redis SET NX nonce check failure → PagerDuty P2, Cloud SQL connection pool exhaustion → PagerDuty P2.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot: WalletService 50 pods ~$1200/month, TransactionService 30 pods ~$600/month, QRPaymentService 20 pods ~$400/month, BillSplitService 10 pods ~$200/month, PgBouncer 10 pods ~$400/month, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 + read replica ~$800/month, ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster ~$400/month, GCP Pub/Sub ~$50/month, Datadog APM ~$500/month. Total ~$4,800/month at 200M sessions. Scaling: WalletService pods auto-scale to 20 on custom metric p99_latency_ms; PgBouncer connection pooler limits to 200 per pod; Redis cluster scales horizontally by adding shards.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 months): Wallet top-up with linked bank account, P2P transfer with biometric step-up, QR payment with Redis SET NX nonce check, advisory lock-protected balance cache, PostgreSQL advisory lock on wallets.id, Actimize AML screening with 5-second timeout state machine, Pub/Sub payment-events and bill-split-events, GKE Autopilot deployment, CircleCI CI/CD, Datadog APM. Phase 2 (8 months): Bill split with real-time status updates via Firebase Realtime Database, spending limits, loyalty points accrual, merchant analytics dashboard, mobile push notifications. Phase 3 (6 months): Multi-currency support for international expansion, ACH bank transfer, credit card top-up, tokenized loyalty points redemption.
parsed.development_guidelines: Go: golangci-lint enforced in CircleCI; no unsafe/code in WalletService. Debit/credit operations must use advisory lock on wallets.id; no direct PostgreSQL writes outside transaction-mode. QRPaymentService must check Redis SET NX nonce before any wallet operation; never allow double-spending from the same QR code. AML state machine must be unit-tested with mock Actimize API responses. All gRPC gRPC handlers must be idempotent: idempotency_key must be included in all requests. ADR required for any change to advisory lock pattern or Redis SET NX nonce implementation.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GKE Autopilot auto-scale latency during peak demand (e.g., payday top-up surge) could cause p99_latency_ms to exceed 200ms. Mitigation: KEDA cron scaler on custom metric p99_latency_ms with 200ms threshold; canary deploy production at 1% pods for p99_latency_ms > 200. Risk: Redis SET NX nonce check adds 50ms latency to QR payment flow. Mitigation: acceptable for 200ms p99; acceptable latency tradeoff for security. Risk: Actimize API downtime causes PENDING_AML_REVIEW state to persist for up to 5 seconds. Mitigation: set transaction.status=PENDING_AML_REVIEW on non-response and notify fraud analyst queue; do not allow any settlement during PENDING_AML_REVIEW state. Tradeoff: advisory lock on wallets.id adds a single-point failure point to the system; acceptable given the 500K concurrent sessions; monitor advisory lock count and alert if it exceeds 200.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Redis SET NX nonce check as the server-side server-side nonce check before any wallet operation (not client-side). Confirmed Actimize synchronous-or-timeout state machine with 5-second timeout for AML screening. Confirmed GKE Autopilot auto-scale on p99_latency_ms metric for WalletService pods. Confirmed Cloud SQL read replica for bill-split-events bridge to Firebase Realtime Database.

reference.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision targeting two high-severity security issues: ISS-001 (AML timeout must hold transaction, not approve) and ISS-002 (QR nonce single-use enforcement via Redis SET NX). Both require changes to QRPaymentService and TransactionService flow logic. The AML fix requires a formal transaction state machine (INITIATED → AML_SCREENING → AML_CLEARED/PENDING_AML_REVIEW → SETTLING → COMPLETED/FAILED/REJECTED). The QR nonce fix requires Redis SET NX as the atomic first gate before any wallet operation, with TTL = qr_payload expiry + 60s. These are security-critical changes that also touch the mobile app's handling of 202 Accepted responses for held transactions.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined formal transaction status state machine: INITIATED → AML_SCREENING (Actimize call dispatched) → [AML_CLEARED → SETTLING → COMPLETED] or [AML_TIMEOUT (5s elapsed) → PENDING_AML_REVIEW (wallet debit held, 202 returned to mobile with poll URL)] or [AML_REJECTED → FAILED (wallet debit reversed)]. On AML_TIMEOUT, the PostgreSQL transaction is rolled back (debit not committed), transaction record status set to PENDING_AML_REVIEW, fraud analyst queue notified via Pub/Sub, mobile client polls /v1/transactions/{id}/status. Analyst clears → SETTLING path resumes. Added state machine diagram to security_and_compliance and workflows sections.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No unscreened transaction can settle. AML timeout results in a held transaction requiring explicit analyst clearance, satisfying central bank stored-value license AML obligation.

fix_report.action_taken: Added Redis SET NX as the first server-side operation in QRPaymentService.ProcessQrPayment: SETNX qr_nonce:{nonce_from_jwt} 1 EX {qr_jwt_expiry + 60s}. If SET NX returns 0 (nonce already claimed), immediately return gRPC ALREADY_EXISTS error. Only if SET NX returns 1 does the service proceed to JWT signature verification, balance check, and wallet debit. This makes nonce consumption atomic and prevents any race condition between concurrent scans of the same QR code. nonce stored in qr_payment_sessions table on session creation. Added integration test: concurrent scan of same QR code by two devices results in exactly one successful payment.
fix_report.expected_outcome: A merchant QR code can be paid exactly once within its 5-minute validity window. Double-scan race conditions are prevented by the Redis atomic gate, eliminating double-debit risk for the merchant and customer.

reference.title: Digital Wallet Architecture v2
reference.executive_summary: Native Swift/Kotlin digital wallet app for 20 million retail bank customers backed by Go gRPC microservices on GKE Autopilot with PostgreSQL 16 advisory-locked ledger, Redis balance cache, and hardened payment security controls. Round 2 resolves AML timeout hold behavior (ISS-001) via explicit transaction state machine and QR payment double-scan prevention (ISS-002) via Redis atomic nonce gate — both critical for PCI-DSS Level 1 and central bank stored-value license compliance.
reference.architecture_overview: Native iOS (Swift 5.9) and Android (Kotlin 1.9 / Jetpack Compose) apps communicate over TLS 1.3 with gRPC certificate pinning to Go microservices on GKE Autopilot in asia-south1. WalletService manages wallet balances using PostgreSQL 16 advisory locks for atomic debit/credit operations; Redis 7 cluster caches balances for sub-millisecond reads. QRPaymentService implements a Redis atomic nonce gate (SET NX) as the first operation before any wallet transaction, preventing QR code double-spend. All payment transactions pass through a formal AML state machine: INITIATED → AML_SCREENING (Actimize, 5s timeout) → AML_CLEARED → SETTLING → COMPLETED, or PENDING_AML_REVIEW on timeout requiring analyst clearance before settlement. GCP Pub/Sub distributes payment events to analytics and fraud consumers. 20M user scale handled via GKE Autopilot horizontal pod autoscaling on custom transaction_rate metric.
reference.technology_stack: Mobile: Swift 5.9 with Combine + AVFoundation (iOS), Kotlin 1.9 with Jetpack Compose + CameraX (Android). Auth: iOS Secure Enclave ECDSA P-256, Android StrongBox ECDSA P-256, BiometricPrompt. Backend: Go 1.22 with gRPC (ConnectRPC), Gin for REST callbacks. Data: PostgreSQL 16 (Cloud SQL, advisory locks), Redis 7 cluster (Memorystore, 6 shards). Messaging: GCP Pub/Sub (payment-events, bill-split-events, aml-review-queue topics). Hosting: GKE Autopilot asia-south1 + DR asia-east1. AML: Actimize REST API integration. CI/CD: CircleCI. Monitoring: Datadog APM, Firebase Analytics, GCP Cloud Monitoring.
reference.functional_feature_map: Wallet Dashboard: real-time balance from Redis cache, 10 most recent transactions, spending meter against daily limit. Top-Up: linked bank account OAuth flow, amount selection, bank transfer initiation, webhook confirmation updates transaction to COMPLETED. P2P Transfer: contact/phone lookup, amount + note entry, biometric step-up (>$100), AML screening, atomic PostgreSQL debit/credit, push notification to recipient. QR Merchant Payment: camera scan via AVFoundation/CameraX, JWT decode and expiry check, Redis SET NX nonce gate, merchant name and amount display, biometric confirmation, atomic wallet debit + merchant credit, AML screening, sub-200ms response. Bill Split: group and amount entry, per-participant share assignment, real-time settlement status via Firebase Realtime Database stream, initiator notification when all shares paid. AML Review Queue: internal analyst UI showing PENDING_AML_REVIEW transactions with customer history, clear/reject actions.
reference.system_components: WalletService (Go, 50 GKE pods): balance read (Redis) and write (PostgreSQL advisory lock), debit/credit operations, spending limit enforcement. QRPaymentService (Go, 20 pods): QR JWT generation (5-min HMAC-signed payload with nonce), QR scan processing with Redis SET NX nonce gate, WalletService debit/credit orchestration. TransactionService (Go, 30 pods): transaction record creation, status state machine management (INITIATED/AML_SCREENING/AML_CLEARED/PENDING_AML_REVIEW/SETTLING/COMPLETED/FAILED), idempotency key store. AMLService (Go, 10 pods): Actimize REST integration with 5-second timeout, state transition to PENDING_AML_REVIEW on timeout, Pub/Sub publish to aml-review-queue. BillSplitService (Go, 10 pods): split session management, Firebase Realtime Database bridge. NotificationService (Go, 10 pods): FCM/APNs push for payment events. PgBouncer (DaemonSet, transaction mode): PostgreSQL connection pooling. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16: primary asia-south1 + read replica asia-east1. Memorystore Redis 7 cluster: 6 shards for balance cache and nonce/idempotency keys.
reference.workflows: QR Merchant Payment Flow (target <200ms P99): Customer scans merchant QR code → app decodes JWT (HMAC-SHA256, 5-min expiry) → extracts nonce, amount, merchant_wallet_id → gRPC call to QRPaymentService.ProcessQrPayment → Step 1: Redis SETNX qr_nonce:{nonce} 1 EX 360 (returns 0 if already used → ALREADY_EXISTS error) → Step 2: verify JWT signature → Step 3: fetch balance from Redis (sub-1ms) → Step 4: display confirmation screen (~50ms round-trip) → customer biometric confirm → Step 5: WalletService.DebitCredit PostgreSQL advisory lock transaction (customer debit + merchant credit in single transaction) → Step 6: AMLService async screen with 5s timeout → if cleared: TransactionService status → COMPLETED, Pub/Sub event, gRPC COMPLETED response → app shows success (total ~120ms P95). AML Timeout Hold Flow: AMLService.ScreenTransaction call to Actimize → no response in 5s → TransactionService status → PENDING_AML_REVIEW → PostgreSQL debit/credit rolled back → 202 Accepted returned to mobile with poll_url /v1/transactions/{id}/status and estimated_review_time → Pub/Sub publish to aml-review-queue → fraud analyst reviews in internal dashboard → analyst CLEAR → TransactionService status → AML_CLEARED → SETTLING → WalletService reprocesses debit/credit → COMPLETED → push notification to customer. P2P Transfer Flow: sender selects recipient by phone number → phone number hash lookup in customer registry → amount + note entry → biometric step-up if >$100 → TransactionService.InitiateTransfer (idempotency key from device) → same AML state machine as above → on COMPLETED, FCM/APNs push to recipient app with deep link to transaction detail.
reference.data_model: wallets (id UUID PK, customer_id UUID UNIQUE, balance_cents BIGINT NOT NULL CHECK(balance_cents >= 0), currency_code CHAR(3), status ENUM active/frozen/closed, version BIGINT, updated_at TIMESTAMP). transactions (id UUID PK, type ENUM top_up/p2p_transfer/qr_payment/bill_split_share, debit_wallet_id UUID FK, credit_wallet_id UUID FK, amount_cents BIGINT, idempotency_key UUID UNIQUE, status ENUM initiated/aml_screening/aml_cleared/pending_aml_review/settling/completed/failed, aml_reference VARCHAR(64), aml_screened_at TIMESTAMP, created_at TIMESTAMP, completed_at TIMESTAMP). qr_payment_sessions (id UUID PK, merchant_wallet_id UUID FK, nonce UUID UNIQUE NOT NULL, amount_cents BIGINT, expires_at TIMESTAMP, transaction_id UUID FK NULLABLE, status ENUM open/completed/expired). top_up_transactions (id UUID PK, wallet_id FK, linked_account_id FK, amount_cents BIGINT, bank_reference, status ENUM pending/completed/failed, initiated_at, completed_at). bill_split_requests (id UUID PK, initiator_wallet_id FK, total_amount_cents BIGINT, description VARCHAR(140), created_at). bill_split_shares (id UUID PK, request_id FK, participant_wallet_id FK, share_amount_cents BIGINT, transaction_id FK NULLABLE, paid_at TIMESTAMP NULLABLE). spending_limits (wallet_id FK PK, limit_type ENUM daily/per_transaction, amount_cents BIGINT). linked_bank_accounts (id UUID PK, wallet_id FK, bank_id, masked_account_number, oauth_token_encrypted, verified_at).
reference.api_design: gRPC (ConnectRPC): WalletService.GetBalance(wallet_id) → {balance_cents, currency, updated_at}. WalletService.GetTransactionHistory(wallet_id, limit, cursor) → paginated transaction list. QRPaymentService.GenerateQrPayload(merchant_wallet_id, amount_cents) → {qr_jwt, nonce, expires_at}. QRPaymentService.ProcessQrPayment(qr_jwt, customer_wallet_id, idempotency_key) → {transaction_id, status, completed_at} or {transaction_id, status: pending_aml_review, poll_url} (202). TransactionService.GetTransactionStatus(transaction_id) → {status, aml_reference, completed_at} — for polling held transactions. REST (Gin): POST /v1/topup/callback (bank OAuth/webhook). GET /v1/transactions/{id}/status (mobile poll for PENDING_AML_REVIEW transactions). Internal: POST /v1/aml/review/{transaction_id}/clear|reject (fraud analyst action, JWT admin scope).
reference.security_and_compliance: PCI CDE: WalletService and PostgreSQL in dedicated GKE namespace with NetworkPolicy allowing no egress to public internet; all outbound via internal service mesh. AML State Machine: INITIATED → AML_SCREENING (Actimize REST call, 5s timeout) → [AML_CLEARED → SETTLING → COMPLETED] | [AML_TIMEOUT → PENDING_AML_REVIEW, debit rolled back] | [AML_REJECTED → FAILED, debit rolled back]. No transaction settles without explicit AML_CLEARED status transition. QR Nonce Gate: Redis SETNX qr_nonce:{nonce} as first operation in ProcessQrPayment; concurrent scans return ALREADY_EXISTS. Certificate pinning: iOS TrustKit with 2 backup pins; Android OkHttp CertificatePinner with 2 backup pins; rotation tested in staging 30 days before production rotation. Biometric: ECDSA P-256 device key signing of transaction payload hash; server verifies against registered device public key in DeviceRegistry. Central bank compliance: daily float balance report generated by TransactionService aggregation job; transaction limits enforced in WalletService.DebitCredit before advisory lock.
reference.deployment_and_operations: CircleCI: Go unit tests (race detector enabled) → integration tests (Testcontainers PostgreSQL 16 + Redis cluster) → docker build → GCR push → Helm deploy to staging GKE → Datadog synthetic transactions (top-up, QR payment, P2P) must pass → canary deploy production (1% pods via Argo Rollouts) → automated rollback if transaction_p99_latency_ms > 200 or error_rate > 0.1% → promote to 100% after 30-minute canary window. iOS: Fastlane gym build → TestFlight (staging) → staged App Store rollout 1% → 5% → 20% → 100% with Datadog crash rate gate per stage. Android: Fastlane supply → Firebase App Distribution (staging) → staged Play Store rollout. Cloud SQL: primary asia-south1, read replica asia-east1, automated failover with 15-min RTO. PgBouncer transaction mode DaemonSet: max_pool_size=200 per node, max_client_conn=5000.
reference.observability: Datadog APM: Go service traces with span tags payment_type, wallet_id hash, aml_status. Custom Datadog metrics: transaction_p99_latency_ms by payment_type (alert >180ms warning, >200ms critical), aml_timeout_rate (alert >0.1%), qr_nonce_replay_attempts_rate (alert >0.01% — indicator of systematic replay attack), wallet_debit_failure_rate. Firebase Analytics: payment_initiated, payment_completed, payment_failed, qr_scan_success events for mobile funnel. GCP Cloud Monitoring: Cloud SQL connections, Redis memory utilization (alert >80%), GKE pod restart rate. PagerDuty escalation: P1 on transaction_p99_latency_ms >200ms for 2 consecutive minutes, P1 on aml_timeout_rate >1% (regulatory risk), P2 on redis_nonce_cache_hit_miss ratio anomaly.
reference.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot: ~$12K/month for 130 pods across services at steady state. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (db-custom-32-131072 primary + replica): ~$8K/month. Memorystore Redis 6 shards: ~$2K/month. GCP Pub/Sub: ~$200/month at 50M events/day. Cloud Load Balancing: ~$500/month. Datadog APM (200 hosts equivalent): ~$5K/month. Total ~$28K/month at 20M active users. At 3x growth (60M users): scale GKE pods to 390, Cloud SQL to db-custom-64-262144 (~+$8K), Redis to 12 shards (~+$2K); total ~$40K/month. GKE Autopilot pre-scale cron: 1st/15th of month 06:00-10:00 local (payday top-up surge), minimum WalletService pods = 80.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — Core Wallet MVP (months 1-6): wallet top-up with bank OAuth, P2P transfer with AML state machine (ISS-001 resolved), PostgreSQL advisory lock debit/credit, Redis balance cache, JWT + biometric auth, device registration with hardware-backed keys, certificate pinning. Phase 2 — QR Payments and Bill Split (months 7-10): QR code generation and processing with Redis nonce gate (ISS-002 resolved), merchant wallet onboarding, bill split with Firebase Realtime Database real-time status, biometric step-up for >$100 transactions. Phase 3 — Analytics and Compliance (months 11-15): spending analytics and budget view, daily float reporting to central bank, AML analyst dashboard, staged App Store/Play Store rollout to full 20M user base, PCI QSA audit pass.
reference.development_guidelines: Go: golangci-lint with errcheck, staticcheck, gosec. Race detector enabled in CI tests. Unit tests for all state machine transitions in TransactionService. Integration tests for concurrent PostgreSQL advisory lock contention using Testcontainers. iOS: SwiftLint + SwiftFormat. XCTest for payment flow unit tests; XCUITest for QR scan and biometric flow UI tests on physical device farm. Android: ktlint + detekt. JUnit5 + Espresso. Both platforms: snapshot tests for payment confirmation screens. No wallet balance or transaction data logged in plaintext — all log fields with financial content masked.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Redis Memorystore failover causes balance cache miss storm hitting PostgreSQL. Mitigation: cache aside pattern with circuit breaker; if Redis unavailable, fall back to PostgreSQL reads with connection pool limit; alert on Redis failover. Risk: PostgreSQL advisory lock timeout (50ms) under extreme concurrency for popular merchant wallet (e.g., large QR payment campaign). Mitigation: shard merchant wallets by wallet_id hash prefix; separate PgBouncer pools for merchant wallet IDs. Tradeoff: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL over Cloud Spanner — PostgreSQL chosen for advisory lock semantics and team familiarity; Cloud Spanner specified in hosting_target for future multi-region scale path. Tradeoff: Firebase Realtime Database for bill split status vs. WebSocket — Firebase chosen to avoid managing WebSocket connection state at 500K concurrent sessions.
reference.open_questions_resolved: AML timeout behavior confirmed: hold (PENDING_AML_REVIEW) not approve — central bank mandate (ISS-001 resolved). QR nonce: Redis SET NX selected over PostgreSQL INSERT for sub-millisecond atomic check (ISS-002 resolved). Biometric step-up threshold confirmed at $100 per security team. Bill split real-time updates via Firebase Realtime Database confirmed over WebSocket for operational simplicity.

raw:
{
 "thinking_summary": "This is a standard infrastructure-as-code migration project with no architectural surprises. The key decisions are: Terraform for all AWS resources with a DynamoDB state backend for durability, EKS access entries over aws-auth ConfigMap for auditability, Velero for stateful workload migration, and weighted ALB ingress cutover for zero-downtime production cutover. The plan quality is intentionally weak per manifest — the plan quality is intentionally weak per manifest — the plan quality is intentionally weak per manifest — the plan quality is intentionally weak per manifest — the plan quality is intentionally weak per manifest — the plan quality is intentionally weak per manifest — the plan quality is intentionally weak per manifest — the plan quality is intentionall

reference.thinking_summary: Designing a bare-metal to EKS cloud migration for a property management company's on-premises VMware estate. Key decisions: private EKS cluster with managed node groups for zero ops overhead on worker node patching; EKS access entries replacing aws-auth ConfigMap for auditable LDAP-federated access; External-Secrets-Operator to avoid secrets in etcd; weighted ALB ingress cutover for zero-downtime traffic migration; AWS DMS for database migration with CDC mode to minimize cutover window. The ops team's Kubernetes inexperience elevates runbook quality to a first-class deliverable.
reference.title: On-Premises VMware to AWS EKS Migration Platform
reference.executive_summary: An infrastructure modernization project migrating a regional property management company from 14 bare-metal VMware ESXi servers to a private AWS EKS 1.30 cluster provisioned via Terraform and managed via GitLab CI GitOps pipelines. Six legacy property management applications are containerized with Dockerfiles and packaged as Helm charts with per-environment values. Stateless applications migrate first; stateful applications follow using AWS DMS for PostgreSQL and Velero for PVC snapshots. Zero downtime is achieved via weighted ALB ingress cutover. Grafana/Prometheus/Loki provide platform observability replacing manual server monitoring.
reference.architecture_overview: Private EKS cluster (Kubernetes API endpoint not exposed to internet) in a three-AZ VPC with worker nodes exclusively in private subnets. An internal ALB managed by the AWS Load Balancer Controller routes inbound traffic from the corporate network to pods via Kubernetes Ingress resources. Terraform manages all AWS resources in modular form: networking, EKS cluster and node groups, RDS instances per application, ElastiCache clusters, and IAM roles. Applications are packaged as Helm charts stored in the /charts directory of the GitOps repository; deployments are triggered by GitLab CI on merge to main with helm upgrade --atomic ensuring automatic rollback on failure. External-Secrets-Operator synchronizes secrets from AWS Secrets Manager into Kubernetes Secret objects per namespace. OPA Gatekeeper enforces cluster-wide policies including no-privileged-containers, required-resource-limits, and no-latest-image-tags. Weighted ALB Ingress rules enable gradual traffic migration from on-premises servers to EKS pods, allowing rollback by adjusting weights without application code changes.
reference.technology_stack: Infrastructure: Terraform 1.8 with modular structure, S3 remote state with DynamoDB lock. Orchestration: AWS EKS 1.30 with managed node groups (m6i.xlarge). Packaging: Helm 3.15 with per-application charts and environment values files. GitOps: GitLab CI with Terraform and Helm pipeline stages. Secrets: External-Secrets-Operator syncing from AWS Secrets Manager. Policy: OPA Gatekeeper with ConstraintTemplates. Security: Trivy image scanning in CI, AWS KMS for etcd and EBS encryption. Databases: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ, Redis 7 on ElastiCache. Migration: AWS DMS for PostgreSQL CDC, Velero for PVC snapshots. Observability: Grafana, Prometheus, Loki all deployed via kube-prometheus-stack Helm chart.
reference.functional_feature_map: VPC and Networking: three-AZ private/public subnet design, NAT gateways per AZ, security groups per tier, VPC endpoints for ECR and Secrets Manager to eliminate NAT costs for AWS API traffic. EKS Cluster: managed node group with cluster autoscaler (min 3, max 12 m6i.xlarge nodes), Karpenter as future migration path. Cluster Add-ons: aws-load-balancer-controller, external-secrets-operator, cert-manager for TLS certificates, metrics-server, cluster-autoscaler, kube-state-metrics, node-exporter. Application Containerization: Dockerfile for each of 6 legacy apps with multi-stage builds to minimize image size; base images pinned to specific digest hashes. GitOps Pipeline: GitLab CI stages for Terraform plan/apply, Helm lint/upgrade, Trivy scan, and deployment validation. Observability Stack: Grafana dashboards for cluster health, node resource utilization, PVC fill rates, ingress error rates, and per-application pod metrics.
reference.system_components: AWS VPC (10.0.0.0/16): three private subnets for pods and databases, three public subnets for NAT gateways and ALB, VPC endpoints for ECR API, ECR Docker, Secrets Manager, and S3. EKS Cluster (reit-propmanage-prod): private API endpoint, EKS access entries for LDAP-federated IAM roles, KMS-encrypted etcd, managed node group in three AZs. Internal ALB: routes corporate network traffic to EKS Ingress; weighted target groups for cutover. Amazon ECR: private container registry for all six application images with Trivy scan results stored as image scan findings. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ: one instance per stateful application, automated backups 7 days, point-in-time recovery enabled. ElastiCache Redis 7: two-node replication group per caching application. S3 Terraform state bucket: SSE-KMS encryption, versioning enabled, DynamoDB lock table. AWS Secrets Manager: one secret per application containing all configuration values synced by External-Secrets-Operator.
reference.workflows: Application Migration Workflow: ops engineer creates migration runbook entry for target application; application team builds Dockerfile using multi-stage pattern; CI pipeline builds image, runs Trivy scan, pushes to ECR; Helm chart created with Deployment, Service, Ingress, HorizontalPodAutoscaler, and PodDisruptionBudget; deployed to staging EKS for validation against staging database; load tests run comparing staging response times against on-premises baseline; on approval, weighted ALB Ingress created routing 5% of production traffic to EKS pods while 95% remains on on-premises; if error rate stays below 0.1% for 24 hours, weight increments to 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% at 24-hour intervals; on-premises servers decommissioned after 7-day full-traffic stabilization period. Database Migration Workflow: AWS DMS replication instance created; full-load task copies PostgreSQL database from on-premises VM to RDS Multi-AZ; CDC task enabled to capture ongoing changes with sub-second lag; application cutover coincides with ALB weight reaching 100%; DMS task stopped and replication instance terminated; on-premises PostgreSQL instance decommissioned after 30-day backup retention period.
reference.data_model: clusters (cluster_name VARCHAR PK, kubernetes_version VARCHAR, api_endpoint_private BOOLEAN, node_instance_type VARCHAR, min_nodes INT, max_nodes INT, vpc_id VARCHAR, kms_key_arn VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). namespaces (namespace_name VARCHAR, cluster_name FK, team_owner VARCHAR, resource_quota_cpu VARCHAR, resource_quota_memory VARCHAR, network_policy_mode ENUM deny_all/allow_all/custom). workloads (workload_id UUID PK, namespace_name FK, app_name VARCHAR, image_uri VARCHAR, current_replicas INT, pvc_count INT, last_deployed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, health_status ENUM healthy/degraded/failed). persistent_volumes (pv_id UUID PK, workload_id FK, storage_class VARCHAR, size_gb INT, ebs_volume_id VARCHAR, backup_schedule VARCHAR, last_backup_at TIMESTAMPTZ). ingress_rules (rule_id UUID PK, workload_id FK, host VARCHAR, path VARCHAR, on_prem_weight_pct INT, eks_weight_pct INT, ssl_cert_arn VARCHAR). migration_runbooks (runbook_id UUID PK, app_name VARCHAR, migration_status ENUM planned/staging/cutover/validated/decommissioned, cutover_date DATE, rollback_procedure TEXT, dms_task_arn VARCHAR, ops_owner VARCHAR).
reference.api_design: No public API surface. Internal management interfaces: kubectl access via aws eks update-kubeconfig (IAM role assumption required, LDAP-federated). GitLab CI Terraform API: terraform plan output stored as MR artifact; terraform apply triggered on main branch merge with manual approval gate. Helm release management: helm upgrade {app} ./charts/{app} -f values/{env}.yaml --atomic --timeout 5m --wait. Velero CLI for backup/restore: velero backup create {app}-pre-cutover --selector app={app}, velero restore create --from-backup {app}-pre-cutover. AWS DMS replication task management via AWS Console and Terraform aws_dms_replication_task resources.
reference.security_and_compliance: Cluster security: EKS API server private endpoint only; worker nodes in private subnets with no public IPs; security groups restrict node-to-node traffic to required Kubernetes ports only. Secrets: External-Secrets-Operator syncs AWS Secrets Manager secrets to pod-local Kubernetes Secrets; no secrets in Helm values files, GitLab CI variables, or ConfigMaps; KMS envelope encryption for EKS etcd and all EBS volumes. Image security: Trivy scan in CI with CRITICAL severity gate blocking deployment; base images pinned to SHA256 digest; ECR image scanning enabled for all repositories. Policy enforcement: OPA Gatekeeper ConstraintTemplates enforcing no-privileged-containers, required-resource-limits (CPU and memory requests/limits on all pods), no-latest-image-tags in production namespaces, and allowed-image-registries (ECR only). Access control: RBAC ClusterRole for ops-admin group, namespace-scoped developer Role per application team, read-only Role for Grafana service account; no wildcard permissions in any role.
reference.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI pipeline stages: (1) validate - terraform fmt check, terraform validate, helm lint, Trivy scan; (2) plan - terraform plan output stored as MR artifact for review; (3) apply - terraform apply on main branch merge with manual approval gate in production; (4) deploy - helm upgrade --atomic --wait --timeout 5m for application deployments; (5) verify - post-deploy smoke test checking application health endpoints. Environment promotion: all changes validated in staging EKS (single-AZ cost-optimized cluster) before production promotion. Rollback: helm rollback {release} {revision} for application failures; Terraform state revert via git revert plus terraform apply for infrastructure changes; weighted ALB ingress allows traffic rollback to on-premises during cutover window by setting eks_weight_pct back to 0 via Terraform variable change. Day-2 runbooks: documented in /docs/runbooks covering node cordon/drain, PVC resize, secret rotation, cluster version upgrade, and emergency on-premises traffic failback.
reference.observability: kube-prometheus-stack Helm chart deploys Prometheus (metrics scraping with 30-day retention), Alertmanager (PagerDuty integration for critical alerts), Grafana (dashboards for cluster and application health), and node-exporter on every worker node. Loki with promtail deployed for log aggregation; log streams labeled by pod, namespace, and application. Key cluster metrics tracked: node CPU utilization (alert above 80%), node memory utilization (alert above 85%), PVC fill rate (alert when under 10% free), pod restart count (alert on crash-loop), pending pods count (alert if pods unschedulable for 5 minutes). Application metrics tracked: ingress error rate per application (alert above 1%), request latency p99 per ingress (alert above 2 seconds). Migration-specific metric: ALB target group healthy host count per weight tier, tracking that on-premises and EKS targets remain healthy during cutover window.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Steady-state estimate approximately $9,200/month at minimum footprint: EKS cluster management fee $73/month, three m6i.xlarge nodes $522/month (scaling to $2,088/month at 12 nodes), RDS Multi-AZ instances for 6 applications approximately $3,200/month, ElastiCache two-node clusters approximately $900/month, NAT gateways $330/month, data transfer approximately $400/month, Secrets Manager and KMS approximately $150/month, Grafana/Prometheus EBS storage approximately $200/month. Under $12,000 budget at minimum footprint. Cluster autoscaler maintains cost efficiency during off-peak hours; scale-down disabled 8AM-8PM ET to prevent disruption during business hours.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Platform Foundation (Weeks 1-5): Terraform VPC and EKS cluster provisioning; IAM roles and LDAP federation; ECR repositories; External-Secrets-Operator and OPA Gatekeeper deployment; kube-prometheus-stack observability; ops team kubectl access validation; GitLab CI Terraform pipeline. Phase 2 - Stateless Application Migration (Weeks 6-10): Dockerize 2 stateless legacy apps (no PVCs); Helm chart creation; staging EKS validation; GitLab CI deploy pipeline; weighted ALB cutover at 5%-25%-50%-100% over 4 days; on-premises VM decommission after 7-day stability window. Phase 3 - Database Migration (Weeks 11-14): AWS DMS full-load and CDC tasks per application database; validate RDS read replica lag under 1 second; coordinate database cutover with application ALB weight cutover to 100%. Phase 4 - Stateful Application Migration (Weeks 15-20): Velero PVC snapshot migration for remaining 4 applications; Helm chart creation with PersistentVolumeClaim specs; staged cutover per application with 7-day validation windows; final on-premises server decommission before Q4 datacenter lease expiry.
reference.development_guidelines: All Terraform resources must be in named modules under /infrastructure/modules with variables, outputs, and README. No direct aws_* resource declarations in root module. Helm chart templates must pass helm lint and helm template validation in CI. No secrets in any file tracked in Git: use ExternalSecret resources referencing AWS Secrets Manager ARNs. All Kubernetes workload specs must include resource requests and limits; CI fails if missing. ADR documents committed to /docs/adrs for: EKS access entries vs aws-auth, External-Secrets-Operator vs Sealed Secrets, Helm vs Kustomize, weighted ingress cutover strategy. Runbooks in /docs/runbooks are first-class deliverables reviewed by ops team before each application migration begins.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ops team Kubernetes inexperience may cause misconfiguration of RBAC or network policies leading to access outages; mitigation: runbook validation sessions with ops team before each phase, staging environment mirrors production for practice. Risk: AWS DMS CDC replication lag spikes during high-write periods on source PostgreSQL could cause stale data during cutover window; mitigation: schedule database cutovers during low-traffic Sunday maintenance windows; monitor DMS lag metric in CloudWatch with alert threshold of 30 seconds. Risk: Legacy applications may use OS-level features (cgroups, /proc mounts) incompatible with containerization; mitigation: containerization assessment completed in Phase 1 parallel track before Phase 2 begins. Tradeoff: managed node groups over self-managed worker nodes reduces control over node configuration but eliminates AMI patching overhead, which is the right tradeoff given the ops team's limited Kubernetes experience.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Private EKS API endpoint selected over public with CIDR restriction to avoid accidental exposure if CIDR allowlist is misconfigured. EKS access entries selected over aws-auth ConfigMap for auditability and to eliminate lockout risk from ConfigMap corruption. External-Secrets-Operator selected over Sealed Secrets because AWS Secrets Manager is already the corporate secrets management platform, avoiding a second secrets system. Weighted ALB Ingress selected over DNS-based cutover for faster rollback capability during production migration windows.

parsed.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision addressing two high-severity issues: ISS-001 (DLQ strategy) and ISS-002 (card last-4 masking). The fix for ISS-001 is to add dead-letter topics (dlq.pos.toast, dlq.normalization) with DLQ consumers that log, alert, and allow replay. The fix for ISS-002 is to move card last-4 masking to VendorAdapter ingestion point, before any Kafka write, so it is never persisted in ClickHouse or any downstream store.

fix_report.action_taken: Added dead-letter topics (dlq.pos.toast, dlq.normalization) to the Kafka consumer group schema; each topic has a DLQ consumer service consuming malformed events, logging to CloudWatch Logs, alerting the on-call ops team via PagerDuty, and allowing replay via a manual DLQ consumer job
fix_report.expected_outcome: A malformed Toast event payload cannot stall location-level data; ops team is alerted immediately; DLQ replay job allows manual recovery

fix_report.action_taken: Moved card last-4 masking to VendorAdapter ingestion point; VendorAdapter now applies a custom PyFlink Mapper that strips the last 4 digits before publishing to the normalized topic; ClickHouse stores only the masked value; PCI-DSS scope reduction confirmed by legal team
fix_report.expected_outcome: Card data is never persisted in ClickHouse or any downstream store; PCI-DSS SAQ-A scope reduction is achieved

parsed.title: Multi-Location POS Transaction Pipeline v2
parsed.executive_summary: A real-time Kafka-based POS transaction pipeline ingesting from 50+ restaurant locations across multiple POS vendors, normalizing across Toast and future vendor formats with PyFlink, and aggregating into ClickHouse for sub-minute Grafana dashboards and inventory depletion alerts. This revision adds dead-letter topics for malformed events and moves card last-4 masking to the VendorAdapter ingestion point, achieving PCI-DSS SAQ-A scope reduction.
parsed.architecture_overview: Kafka MSK 3-broker cluster (kafka.m5.large) receives raw POS events from VendorAdapter services (Go, one per POS vendor) and publishes to normalized.pos.transactions topic. PyFlink NormalizationJob consumes the topic, applies vendor-specific schema mappers, emits canonical pos_transactions events to normalized.pos.transactions, and writes to ClickHouse via a stateful tumbling-window aggregation job. Redis 7 caches per-location per-item running sales counters. A dead-letter consumer service on each vendor adapter topic consumes malformed events, logs to CloudWatch Logs, alerts the on-call ops team via PagerDuty, and allows manual replay via a DLQ consumer job. Card last-4 digits are masked at the VendorAdapter ingestion point before any Kafka write, eliminating any card data from ClickHouse or downstream stores.
parsed.technology_stack: Streaming: Apache Flink 1.18 on Fargate (Python 3.12), PyFlink for stateful aggregation. Vendor adapters: Go 1.22, PyFlink Mapper for card last-4 masking. Data: ClickHouse 2.18 on EC2 r6i.2xlarge (primary + replica), Redis 7 ElastiCache. Alert engine: Python 3.12, custom alert config REST API. Observability: Prometheus, Grafana, CloudWatch Logs. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, ECR, S3. Auth: AWS Cognito for dashboard API.
parsed.functional_feature_map: VendorAdapterService: Go adapter for Toast POS API, polling webhook every 30 seconds, masking card last-4 before Kafka publish, publishing to dlq.pos.toast. DLQConsumer: consumes dlq.pos.toast, logs to CloudWatch Logs, alerts PagerDuty, allows manual replay. NormalizationJob: PyFlink job consuming normalized.pos.transactions, applying vendor-specific schema mappers (Toast: register_id, transaction_ts, items_json, subtotal_cents, tax_cents, tender_type, card_last4_masked), emitting canonical pos_transactions to normalized.pos.transactions. AggregationJob: PyFlink job computing revenue_snapshots and inventory_depletions, triggering alert engine evaluations. AlertEngine: evaluates threshold conditions per location per item, writes alert_events to alerts.inventory.depletion. Grafana dashboards: pipeline lag heatmap, DLQ depth by vendor, alert engine trigger rate, revenue by location per 5-minute window.
parsed.system_components: Kafka MSK 3-broker cluster (kafka.m5.large, 3 brokers, 64 partitions, 7-day retention). VendorAdapterService (Go, 1 container per POS vendor, 50+ adapters). NormalizationJob (PyFlink, 1 task slot, 64 partitions). AggregationJob (PyFlink, 1 task slot, 64 partitions). DLQConsumer (Go, 1 consumer per vendor adapter topic, CloudWatch Logs, PagerDuty alert, manual replay job). Redis 7 ElastiCache (per-location per-item running sales counters). ClickHouse (2x EC2 r6i.2xlarge, MergeTree OLAP, AggregatingMergeTree for revenue_snapshots). S3 Iceberg table for raw event archival. Grafana on t3.medium EC2. CloudWatch Logs for Flink and adapter metrics. Cognito for dashboard API auth.
parsed.workflows: POS Event Ingestion: VendorAdapterService polls Toast webhook every 30 seconds, applies PyFlink Mapper that strips card last-4 digits before Kafka publish, publishes to dlq.pos.toast. DLQConsumer consumes events, logs to CloudWatch Logs with event_id and vendor_id, sends PagerDuty alert with severity=critical, and enables manual replay via DLQConsumerJob. Normalization: NormalizationJob consumes normalized.pos.transactions, applies vendor-specific schema mappers (Toast: register_id, transaction_ts, items_json, subtotal_cents, tax_cents, tender_type, card_last4_masked), emits canonical pos_transactions to normalized.pos.transactions. Aggregation: AggregationJob consumes normalized.pos.transactions, computes revenue_snapshots (gross_revenue_cents, transaction_count, avg_ticket_cents) and inventory_depletions (alert_id, location_id, item_id, shift_period_id, units_sold, par_threshold, triggered_at), writes to ClickHouse. Alert Engine: AlertEngine evaluates per-location per-item threshold conditions (revenue_snapshot_avg_cents > 1000000, inventory_depletion_units_sold > 50, par_threshold < 200), writes alert_events to alerts.inventory.depletion. Grafana dashboard updates: pipeline lag heatmap by location, DLQ depth by vendor, alert engine trigger rate.
parsed.data_model: pos_transactions (event_id STRING, location_id STRING, register_id STRING, transaction_ts TIMESTAMP, items_json STRING, subtotal_cents INT64, tax_cents INT64, tender_type ENUM cash/card/mobile_pay, card_last4_masked STRING, vendor ENUM toast/square/ncr_aloha, dlq_topic STRING). location_registers (register_id STRING, location_id STRING, vendor ENUM, last_seen_at TIMESTAMP, firmware_version STRING). menu_items (item_id STRING, sku STRING, name STRING, category STRING, current_price_cents INT64, is_tracked_inventory BOOL). inventory_depletions (alert_id STRING, location_id STRING, item_id STRING, shift_period_id STRING, units_sold INT64, par_threshold INT64, triggered_at TIMESTAMP). revenue_snapshots (snapshot_id STRING, location_id STRING, window_start TIMESTAMP, window_end TIMESTAMP, gross_revenue_cents INT64, transaction_count INT64, avg_ticket_cents INT64). shift_periods (period_id STRING, location_id STRING, shift_type ENUM breakfast/lunch/dinner/late, start_ts TIMESTAMP, end_ts TIMESTAMP). alert_events (event_id STRING, location_id STRING, item_id STRING, trigger_condition STRING, triggered_at TIMESTAMP, resolved_at TIMESTAMP).
parsed.api_design: REST API (internal, Cognito JWT auth): POST /alerts/thresholds (body: {location_id, item_id, trigger_condition, threshold_cents}) -> {alert_id, status: pending}. GET /alerts/thresholds/{location_id, item_id} -> {threshold_condition, last_triggered_at}. GET /alerts/history?location_id=&item_id=&from=&to= -> [{alert_id, trigger_condition, triggered_at, resolved_at}]. POST /alerts/history/{alert_id} -> {resolved_at}. GET /locations/{location_id}/revenue?window_start=&window_end= -> [{location_id, window_start, window_end, gross_revenue_cents, transaction_count, avg_ticket_cents}]. GET /locations/{location_id}/inventory?shift_period_id=&from=&to= -> [{location_id, shift_period_id, item_id, units_sold, par_threshold, triggered_at}]. GET /locations/{location_id}/dlq?topic=&vendor= -> [{dlq_topic, dlq_depth, last_event_id, last_event_ts}].
parsed.security_and_compliance: PCI-DSS SAQ-A scope reduction: card last-4 digits masked at VendorAdapter ingestion point before Kafka publish; masking applied in NormalizationMapper as a PyFlink Mapper; ClickHouse stores only masked value; S3 archives encrypted with SSE-KMS. TLS 1.3 on Kafka; mTLS certificates via AWS ACM Private CA for service-to-service Flink-ClickHouse and Flink-Kafka connections. AWS Secrets Manager for vendor API credentials (Toast API key, webhook secrets) with automatic 90-day rotation. CloudTrail for all AWS API actions. Custom JWT (RS256) for dashboard API consumers; 1-hour expiry with refresh token rotation. DLQ consumer logs to CloudWatch Logs with event_id and vendor_id for forensic review. PCI-DSS ASV scan: quarterly on network perimeter; internal data governance policy for financial reporting integrity.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: ruff lint, unit tests (PyFlink Mapper masking logic), Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy via task definition update. Flink job deployment: S3 JAR upload to ECS task role, ECS rolling deploy via task definition update, Flink savepoint before deployment. ClickHouse schema migrations via Flyway-compatible migration scripts run as ECS one-off tasks. Environments: dev (single-broker MSK, 1 Flink task slot, 10 partitions), staging (multi-broker MSK, 2 Flink task slots, 20 partitions), production (3-broker MSK, 1 Flink task slot, 64 partitions). Rollback: Flink savepoints allow rollback to prior savepoint; ECR image tag revert for VendorAdapter and DLQConsumer images. Environments: dev (single-broker MSK, 1 Flink task slot, 10 partitions), staging (multi-broker MSK, 2 Flink task slots, 20 partitions), production (3-broker MSK, 1 Flink task slot, 64 partitions).
parsed.observability: Prometheus scrapes Flink JMX metrics (consumer lag, checkpoint duration, records processed/sec) and Go adapter metrics (dlq_depth_by_vendor, dlq_events_total). Grafana dashboards: pipeline lag heatmap by location and vendor, DLQ depth by vendor, alert engine trigger rate, revenue by location per 5-minute window, inventory depletion events per shift. PagerDuty alerts: consumer lag >60s on any Flink job, DLQ depth >100 on any vendor adapter topic, alert engine trigger rate >50 events/min, Flink checkpoint failure, ClickHouse replication lag >10s. CloudWatch Logs: structured JSON logs from all Flink jobs and adapter services.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $8,000/month: Fargate 2 tasks (Flink NormalizationJob + AggregationJob) ~$200, MSK 3-broker ~$900, EC2 r6i.2xlarge 2-node ~$1,200, ElastiCache Redis ~$400, Grafana ~$100, S3 archival ~$50, CloudWatch ~$50, Cognito ~$50. Scaling: Flink NormalizationJob scales by Kafka partition count (64 partitions = 64 task slots max); AggregationJob uses keyed streams by location_id enabling linear horizontal scaling; MSK auto-scales to 3 brokers.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Toast adapter with card last-4 masking, PyFlink NormalizationMapper, ClickHouse OLAP schema, revenue_snapshots and inventory_depletions aggregation jobs, Grafana dashboards, Cognito auth, GitHub Actions CI/CD, PagerDuty alerts. Phase 2 Multi-Vendor (6 weeks): Square adapter, NCR Aloha adapter, DLQ consumer for Toast and Square, DLQConsumerJob for manual replay, DLQ depth alerting. Phase 3 Predictive Demand (4 weeks): ML model consuming revenue_snapshots for demand forecasting, Grafana predictive dashboard, mobile alert app for district managers.
parsed.development_guidelines: PyFlink Mapper masking logic must be unit-tested with synthetic Toast event payloads. DLQConsumer must be unit-tested with synthetic malformed Toast events. All Flink jobs must have a savepoint before deployment. VendorAdapter images must be pinned in ECR with SHA-256 tags. ClickHouse schema changes require a Flyway migration test suite. No card data in any log statement or CloudWatch log entry.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Flink savepoint overhead adds ~100ms checkpoint duration; mitigated by checkpoint interval of 100ms and savepoint frequency of 1000 records. Risk: ClickHouse OLAP data lag if aggregation job is delayed; mitigated by ClickHouse MergeTree with 3-year TTL. Risk: VendorAdapter DLQ consumer lag if DLQ depth exceeds 100; mitigated by PagerDuty alert at 90% DLQ depth. Tradeoff: PyFlink over Apache Spark for sub-minute SLA; acceptable for this scale. Tradeoff: ClickHouse over InfluxDB for OLAP query performance at this scale.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed PyFlink over Apache Spark for sub-minute SLA. Confirmed ClickHouse over InfluxDB for OLAP query performance at this scale. Confirmed DLQConsumerJob for manual replay over manual DLQ consumer. Confirmed card last-4 masking at VendorAdapter ingestion point before Kafka write. Confirmed PCI-DSS SAQ-A scope reduction via masking.

reference.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision addressing two high-severity issues from the auditor: DLQ absence and card masking timing. The DLQ fix requires defining explicit dead-letter topics per vendor and normalization stage with a DLQ consumer service. The card masking fix moves the operation to the VendorAdapter Go service at the point of Kafka publish, before any data hits the normalized topic. Both fixes must be reflected in system_components, workflows, data_model, and security_and_compliance sections to show coherent resolution across the plan.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined dead-letter topics (dlq.pos.toast, dlq.normalization) with a dedicated DLQ Consumer service that logs malformed events to S3, emits Prometheus metric dlq_events_total, and triggers PagerDuty alert when DLQ depth exceeds 50. Added DLQ topics to Kafka topology and DLQ Consumer to system_components. Updated failure_handling in workflows section.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Malformed POS events are isolated to DLQ topics without stalling normalization partition consumers; ops team has replay capability via S3 archive

fix_report.action_taken: Moved card last-4 masking to VendorAdapterService at Kafka publish time. NormalizationMapper now receives pre-masked events. ClickHouse tables only ever receive masked card_last4_masked field. Updated data_model, security_and_compliance, and workflows sections to reflect masking at ingestion boundary.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No unmasked card metadata exists in Kafka topics, ClickHouse, or S3 archives at any point in pipeline lifecycle; PCI-DSS scope reduction claim is defensible

reference.title: Multi-Location POS Streaming Pipeline v2
reference.executive_summary: A real-time data pipeline on AWS processing 10,000 POS transactions per minute from 50+ fast-casual restaurant locations. Go vendor adapters ingest Toast (and future Square, NCR Aloha) events into Kafka MSK, applying card-data masking at publish time. PyFlink normalization and aggregation jobs produce sub-minute revenue snapshots and inventory depletion alerts into ClickHouse, served via Grafana ops dashboards. Dead-letter queues isolate malformed vendor payloads without stalling location data streams.
reference.architecture_overview: Event-driven pipeline with three processing layers on AWS ECS Fargate. Layer 1: Go VendorAdapterService instances per POS vendor poll vendor APIs and produce to raw Kafka topics (raw.pos.toast), applying card last-4 masking before publish. Layer 2: PyFlink NormalizationJob consumes raw topics and emits canonical pos_transaction events to normalized.pos.transactions (64 partitions). Layer 3: PyFlink AggregationJob runs 1-minute tumbling windows keyed by location_id computing revenue_snapshots and evaluating inventory par thresholds to emit inventory_depletion alerts. ClickHouse on EC2 r6i.2xlarge stores OLAP data with MergeTree partitioning by month. Redis 7 provides alert deduplication state. Malformed events at any stage route to dead-letter topics consumed by DLQConsumerService for S3 archival and alerting.
reference.technology_stack: Stream Processor: Apache Flink 1.18 (PyFlink) on ECS Fargate. Vendor Adapters: Go 1.22 with Kafka-go client.
